Blancan component of mixed Blancan-Rancholabrean fauna; based on Morgan and White 1995 some of the "Blancan" taxa may still be misidentified; however, only Castoroides is listed by Webb and explicitly stated to be Rancholabrean by Morgan and Hulbert 1995
Webb's "IB" is actually a composite of river bottom (1A) and in-place Blancan river bank (1B) deposits: Morgan and Ridgway 1987
"Castoroides ohioensis" ("C. sp." of Morgan and Ridgway 1987) is believed to be a Rancholabrean specimen by Morgan and White 1995

"Trigonictis sp." is not present: Ray et al. 1981, p. 8

"Mammut americanum" is omitted by Morgan and Hulbert 1995 and presumably is Rancholabrean

"Mammuthus sp." of Lambert 1995 from Santa Fe River 1B is by Cassiliano 1999 to be of doubtful assignment to the Blancan faunal component, even though the material was said by Lambert to be in situ

primary_reference: M. L. Hopkins, R. Bonnichsen, and D. Fortsch. 1969. The stratigraphic position and faunal associates of Bison (Gigantobison) latifrons in southeastern Idaho, a progress report. Tebiwa 12(1):1-8
taxonomy_comments:
Cedar Butte Basalt directly overlies Bed E and was dated at 0.072 +/- 0.014 Ma (whole-rock KA) by Scott et al. 1982; in turn, the underlying basalt of Crystal Springs was dated at 0.210 Ma (KA) by Desborough et al. 1989, as reported by Pinsof 1998
Sangamonian, and overlying B layer is middle Wisconsinan: Pinsof 1991
B layer fauna from Bronco Jim Point and Rainbow Beach is described by McDonald and Anderson 1975 and called the Rainbow Beach fauna by Kurten and Anderson 1980
Carr-Trimble site (Duck Point) is Wisconsinan according to White 1975 and Kurten and Anderson 1980, and therefore records for this site only are excluded
all rodents and lagomorphs reported by Hopkins et al. 1969 were from the Carr-Trimble site
all specimens are ISUM; early collection of Gazin 1935b is not discussed
Bison antiquus is present, but in the B layer only: Pinsof 1991
Pinsof 1998 states that there are "approximately 6,000 specimens" (meaning of vertebrates); of these, he lists 3197 belonging to generically determinate mammals, not including 1771 specifically indeterminate bison specimens

lithdescript.1: "mossy peat with sandy partings, a few cobbles and boulders, larch trunks... and many beaver-cut sticks" (Hutchison and Harington 2002)
geology_comments: "The pond appears to have been located in open larch woodland, near the tree line of the time": 15º C winter temperatures and 10º C summer temperatures (Dawson and Harington 2007)
collectors: J. G. Fyles, C. R. Harington, J. S. Tener

geogcomments: Located about 9 km north of Lufeng, at the southern side of Miaoshanpo-Mountain; IVPP-Point 75033, called section D.
stratcomments: Quarry sequence is divided into 8 beds (numbered from top), in the Xi River Basin north of Lufeng, China. These unconformably overly Mesozoic sediments. No Formation names are given.\r\nHan 1985: "Its geological age is Late Miocene, equivalent to the Turolian of European sequence of land mammal ages." Qi 1986: Age "8 Ma or perhaps a bit later"
lithdescript: Quarry section at Lufeng is 8 m thick. Detailed microstratigraphy given in Qi 1985 and Badgley et al 1988.
primary_reference: G. Qi. 1985. A preliminary report on Carnivora from the Ramapithecus locality, Lufeng, Yunnan. Acta Anthropologica Sinica (Renleixue xuebao) 4(1):33-43
lithdescript.1: Quarry section at Lufeng is 8 m thick. Detailed microstratigraphy given in Qi 1985 and Badgley et al 1988.

geogcomments: 10 km from present day seashore, on SE border of Casablanca. Lat/long unpublished, assigned from Casablanca
localbed: M1, M2, D2, C1, C2 or LR
stratcomments: Biostratigraphic comparison of fauna suggests age of ~2.5Ma, >2.0Ma for NaO
lithdescript: M1: biocalcarenite. M2: conglomerate. D2: sands. C1: sands among rockfalls. C2: calcareous crust. LR: sands and silts.
primary_reference: J. P. Raynal, J. P. Texier, and D. Geraads, F. Z. Sbihi-Alaoui. 1990. Un nouveau gisement paleontologique plio-pleistocene en Afrique du Nord: Ahl al Oughlam (ancienne carriere Deprez) a Casablanca (Maroc). C. R. Acad. Sci. Paris, Serie II 310:315-320
lithdescript.1: M1: biocalcarenite. M2: conglomerate. D2: sands. C1: sands among rockfalls. C2: calcareous crust. LR: sands and silts.
geology_comments: Marine and karstic cave deposits. Temperate climate, open vegetation, moist.
taxonomy_comments: much material remains unpublished.

geogcomments: On the southern slope of Somleu Hill, near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Cave deposit. The abundance of Mimomys species and the appearance of two primitive species of arhizodont arvicolids make it possible to place this fauna at the beginning of the Biharian (Early Pleistocene, Eburonian). The large number of Villanyian survivors justifies such a dating. Is subsequent to the first Eburonian cooling, and probably corresponds to a warming phase in the middle of this glacial complex.
lithdescript: A calcareous, slightly cemented breccia with terra-rossa, accumulated in a karstic fissure.
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
lithdescript.1: A calcareous, slightly cemented breccia with terra-rossa, accumulated in a karstic fissure.
geology_comments: A calcareous, slightly cemented breccia with terra-rossa, accumulated in a karstic fissure.
collection_comments: Caves discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museum of Oradea.
taxonomy_comments: Contains some thermophilous forms, such as Macaca sylvana cf. florentina and a species of flying squirrel, is subsequent to the first Eburonian cooling, and probably corresponds to a warming phase in the middle of this glacial complex.

geogcomments: SE from Auch, near Castelnau-Barbarens. Coordinates are for Sansan, about 8 km SW from Castelnau-Barbarens.
lithdescript: The fossils come from different, superposed lithologies. Those include: purple clays, gray clays, marls, but also limestones and molasse. No distinction is made about the exact provenance of the fossils from these various levels.
primary_reference: L. Ginsburg. 1964. Les mammiferes fossiles recoltes a Sansan au cours du XIXe siecle. Bulletin de la Societe geologique de France 5(1):3-15
lithdescript.1: The fossils come from different, superposed lithologies. Those include: purple clays, gray clays, marls, but also limestones and molasse. No distinction is made about the exact provenance of the fossils from these various levels.
taxonomy_comments: The ref. Ginsburg (1964)is an update of previous works (Lartet 1836-1858) and Filhol (1891), but it was published as new excavations were taking place.

geogcomments: SE from Auch, near Castelnau-Barbarens. Coordinates are for Sansan, about 8 km SW from Castelnau-Barbarens.
lithdescript: The fossils come from different, superposed lithologies. Those include: purple clays, gray clays, marls, but also limestones and molasse. No distinction is made about the exact provenance of the fossils from these various levels.

lithdescript.1: The fossils come from different, superposed lithologies. Those include: purple clays, gray clays, marls, but also limestones and molasse. No distinction is made about the exact provenance of the fossils from these various levels.
taxonomy_comments: The ref. Ginsburg (1964)is an update of previous works (Lartet 1836-1858) and Filhol (1891), but it was published as new excavations were taking place.

geogcomments: On the Southern slope of Somleu Hill, near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Site overview: The site of Betfia 7 has a height of 8m and encompasses four complexes of layers. The first three of these represent the filling of a former cave, containing numerous fragments of speleothemes. Levels 1b-e have been dated to the late Waalian (MIddle Lower Pleistocene, approx. 1.2-1.15Ma).
lithdescript: the filling of a former cave, containing numerous fragments of speleothemes.
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
lithdescript.1: the filling of a former cave, containing numerous fragments of speleothemes.
collection_comments: Sites discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museumn of Oradea.
taxonomy_comments: Taking into account, on the one hand the progressive dental structure of Microtus pliocaenicus and Prolagurus praepannonicus, and on the other the high frequency of Glirids and the emergence of some thermophilus species, we have ascribed the middle and upper parts of this sequence of strata (levels 1b-e) to an interval with a milder climate, corresponding to the late Waalian.

geogcomments: On the southern slope of Somleu Hill, near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Late Menapian (=Late Lower Pleistocene, approx. 1.05-1.00Ma)
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
collection_comments: Sites discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museum of Oradea
taxonomy_comments: A rich fauna characterised by a marked turnover, due both to the presence of immigrants among the large mammals and to accelerating evolutionary changes in the different lineages of rodents. The high frequency of Leporidae, Cricetidae and lagurines, the prevalence of the genus Microtus and the scarcity of forest species, lead us to assign this fauna to a dry phase. The evolutionary level allows us to place it in the late Menapian.

geogcomments: On the southern slope of Somleu Hill, near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Late Menapian (=Late Lower Pleistocene, approx. 1.05-1.00Ma)
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
collection_comments: Sites discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museum of Oradea

The high frequency of Leporidae, Cricetidae and lagurines, the prevalence of the genus Microtus and the scarcity of forest species, lead us to assign this fauna to a dry phase. The evolutionary level allows us to place it in the late Menapian.

geogcomments: On the southern slope of Somleu Hill, near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Site overview: the site of Betfia has a height of 8m and encompasses four complexes of layers. The last (level 4) is made up of loess-like deposits, accumulated in sub-aerial conditions. Level 4 comprises two layers of loess separated by a fossil soil. Level 4a is the first loess level and is correlated with Glacial A of the Cromerian (= Early Middle Pleistocene, approx. 0.7Ma)
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
geology_comments: The last (level 4) is made up of loess-like deposits, accumulated in sub-aerial conditions. Level 4 comprises two layers of loess separated by a fossil soil. Level 4a is the first loess level.
collection_comments: SItes discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museum of Oradea.
taxonomy_comments: Level 4a, has remarkable faunal diversity, with Mimomys savini, Microtus gregaloides and a high frequency of Bison schotensacki and the first emergence of Microtus agrestis, Ursus deningeri and Mammuthus trogontherii.

geogcomments: on the southern slope of Somleu Hill near the village of Betfia (county Bihor).
stratcomments: Site overview: the site of Betfia 7 has a height of 8m and encompasses four complexes of layers. The last (level 4) is made up of loess-like deposits accumlated in subaerial conditions. Level 4 comprises two layers of loess separated by a fossil soil. Level 4c is the second loess level and is correlated with glacial B of the Cromerian (= Middle Pleistocene, very roughly approx. 0.6Ma).
primary_reference: E. Terzea. 1996. Biochronology of the Pleistocene deposits at Betfia (Bihor, Romania). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):531-540
geology_comments: The last (level 4) is made up of loess-like deposits accumlated in subaerial conditions. Level 4 comprises two layers of loess separated by a fossil soil. Level 4c is the second loess level
collection_comments: Sites discovered between 1966 and 1991 and excavated by the Bucharest Speleological Institute and the "Tarii Crisurilor" Museum of Oradea.
taxonomy_comments: relatively poor fauna, first appearance of Lagurus transiens and a large number of open land dwellers.

geogcomments: 20km south of Liege, at the southwestern end of Sprimont commune. The site is on the right side of the River Ambleve south of Fraiture, approx. 2km from the confluence of the Ambleve and the Ourthe rivers near Comblain-au-Pont.
stratcomments: "the cave appears in cross-section in the upper face of the Quarry called La Belle-Roche". Six levels identfied within the cave deposits, two of which contain faunal remains - 3 "Unsorted silty detrital complex" and 4 "Clay-calcite run-off complex". Four galleries have been found, only Gallery one lacks fauna and archaeology. Dated later than the Brunhes/Matuyama boundary (0.78Ma) and before 0.35Ma (the limit of Uranium/Thorium dating). Thought to be c.0.5Ma on faunal grounds. OIS stage 13 to (perhaps)15.
lithdescript: Level 3 : "silty but progressively loaded from bottom to top wuith reddish decalcification clay and limestone rock fragments". Occasional reworked concretions, probably a secondary mud flow deposits. Bones found from bottom to top, rarely in anatomical position.\r\nLevel 4: "a complex of very thin interbedded layers of calcite, clay and clayey silt as well as a variable succession of laminar calcitic deposits and clayey run-off deposits" Lower layers contain lenses of microfauna, particularly Chiroptera. Higher levels are sterile. Laid down in a very humid period.
primary_reference: J-M. Cordy, B. Bastin, and C. Ek, R. Geeraerts, A. Ozer, Y. Quinif, J. Thorez, M. Ulrix-Closset. 1992. La Belle-Roche (Sprimont, Belgium): the oldest archaeological site in the Benelux. A Report on a field trip.  In M. Toussaint (ed.), FIve Million Years, the Human adventure. Liege: E.R.A.U.L.. 56:287-301
lithdescript.1: Level 3 : "silty but progressively loaded from bottom to top wuith reddish decalcification clay and limestone rock fragments". Occasional reworked concretions, probably a secondary mud flow deposits. Bones found from bottom to top, rarely in anatomical position.\r\nLevel 4: "a complex of very thin interbedded layers of calcite, clay and clayey silt as well as a variable succession of laminar calcitic deposits and clayey run-off deposits" Lower layers contain lenses of microfauna, particularly Chiroptera. Higher levels are sterile. Laid down in a very humid period.
geology_comments: Karstic cave complex orientated East-West along "the stratification of carboniferous limestone beds".
collection_comments: By 1990 400m2 of rock excavated in salvage operations.
taxonomy_comments: Faunal remains are reported from 2 different levels, but the faunal list given here is for the site as a whole. Layer 3 is aperiod of climatic transistion, from cold and continental (glacial) in the lowest level (3a) to warming, sylvan species and large numbers of chiroptera in 3c and level 4. Pollen data also confim this.\r\nlist includes unidentified fish and frog remains.

geogcomments: Vero, on the Atlantic Coast, Florida.
stratcomments: Stratum 3 of Vero excavation produced most materials (= "muck bed"); Stratum 2 less productive but supposedly not significantly older\r\nplants are mostly from layer No. 3 and vertebrates are mostly from layer No. 2 according to Hrdlicka 1917
lithdescript: "The former [human skeleton] lay in dark and somewhat indurated sands, layer No. 2 of Sellards, the latter for the most part at the base of layer No. 3, the muck deposit of the stream bed... layer No. 2 is seen to be overlain by a stratum of similar, but somewhat lighter, sandy deposits covered by a layer of marl [ranging] from about 5 to 9 inches in thickness, and when freshly exposed is of the consistency of fresh mortar, but on exposure hardens to fairly solid rock... The second skeleton lay... in some loose white sand and vegetable matter at the base of the muck layer" (Hrdlicka 1917)
primary_reference: J. Alroy. 2005. Synonymies and reidentifications of North American fossil vertebrates.
lithdescript.1: "The former [human skeleton] lay in dark and somewhat indurated sands, layer No. 2 of Sellards, the latter for the most part at the base of layer No. 3, the muck deposit of the stream bed... layer No. 2 is seen to be overlain by a stratum of similar, but somewhat lighter, sandy deposits covered by a layer of marl [ranging] from about 5 to 9 inches in thickness, and when freshly exposed is of the consistency of fresh mortar, but on exposure hardens to fairly solid rock... The second skeleton lay... in some loose white sand and vegetable matter at the base of the muck layer" (Hrdlicka 1917)
geology_comments: "The lower sand [= Stratum 2] marks the recession of the sea in which the underlying shell marl was formed. The upper beds (No. 3) mark successive seasonal layers of valley filling in the narrow valley of a small stream. The stream was apparently always small."

collection_aka: Th1-G, Hominid Level, H. erectus cave
geogcomments: South of Casablanca
lithdescript: hard pink filling overlain by stalagmitic layers
primary_reference: D. Geraads. 2002. Plio-Pleistocene mammalian biostratigraphy of Atlantic Morocco. Quaternaire 13(1):43-53
lithdescript.1: hard pink filling overlain by stalagmitic layers
collection_comments: Collections made in 1993-1995 yielded one Homo erectus tooth. The earlier 'Thomas Quarry 1' mandible also came from this level.

geogcomments: south of Casablanca. Completely destroyed by quarrying.
stratcomments: This site, formerly known as Thomas Quarry 3, has been completely destroyed by quarrying. Probably belongs to the same faunal unit as Thomas Quarry 1 (very approx. 0.7-0.6Ma).
primary_reference: D. Geraads. 2002. Plio-Pleistocene mammalian biostratigraphy of Atlantic Morocco. Quaternaire 13(1):43-53
taxonomy_comments: no microfauna found at the site, but cranial fragments of Homo erectus were found.

geogcomments: South of Casablanca
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene, approx 0.4Ma. there are 13 different layers within the cave filling, only one of which contains fauna (CGDR or continental 'Grotte des Rhinocéros').
primary_reference: D. Geraads. 2002. Plio-Pleistocene mammalian biostratigraphy of Atlantic Morocco. Quaternaire 13(1):43-53
collection_comments: Discovered in 1991 in a working quarry. Stone tool assemblage contains several thousand artefacts.
taxonomy_comments: Abundant rhinoceros remains. Arid environment rodents and large grazing mammals suggest an open, dry landscape.

geogcomments: 1.5km south of Seydisehir.
stratcomments: Fossils are from a fissure filling some 5m across in 'late Triassic bituminous limestones which outcrop in the northern foothills of the Taurus chain'. Mimomys - Arvicola association suggests (from European comparisons) a late Biharian date, so site tentatively assigned to Middle Pleistocene. Ursus aff. deningeri is also present.
primary_reference: S. Sen, L. de Bonis, and N. Dalfes, D. Geraads, JJ Jaeger, JM Mazin. 1991. Premiere decouverte d'un site a mammiferes pleistocenes dans une fissure karstique en Anatolie centrale. Comptes Rendus de l'Academie des Sciences, Paris, Serie II 313:127-132
taxonomy_comments: Abundant small mammals. Collection also contains amphibians, reptiles and birds. Specimens identified as Canid or Hyaenid indet. are also reported. Fauna suggests a humid and partly forested local environment with some permanent water.

geogcomments: Southern Tadjikistan
stratcomments: Upper Pliocene, Middle Villafranchian, MN17. In the Kuruksay formation, at the base of the loess-palaeosol formation that appears to start at 2.5Ma in China. Palaeomagnetic data indicate the lower part of the Matuyama Chron.
primary_reference: M.V. Sotnikova, A.E. Dodonov, and AV Pen'kov. 1997. Upper Cenozoic bio-magnetic stratigraphy of Central Asian mammalian localities. Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology 133:243-258
collection_comments: at the base of the loess-palaeosol formation, indicating a climatic change to arid conditions.

geogcomments: In the southeastern foothills of Karategin, 8-9km northwest of the city of Faisabad (on which the co-ordinates are based) in the River Djondody valley in the Afghan-Tadjik depression. The bone bearing deposits are in a narrow gorge on the right bank of the River Djondody.
stratcomments: Upper Pliocene, Middle Villafranchian, MN17. In the Kuruksay formation, at the base of the loess-palaeosol formation that appears to start at 2.5Ma in China. Palaeomagentic data indicate the lower part of the Matuyama Chron.
primary_reference: M.V. Sotnikova, A.E. Dodonov, and AV Pen'kov. 1997. Upper Cenozoic bio-magnetic stratigraphy of Central Asian mammalian localities. Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology 133:243-258
collection_comments: Two localities, one in the upper part of the river, another in the lower.

geogcomments: On the left slope of the valley of the River Obigarm, at the mouth of the tributary Deshljashkhar, Afghan-Tadzhik Depression, Southern Tadjikistan.
stratcomments: Upper Pliocene, Middle Villafranchian, MN17. In the Kuruksay formation, at the base of the loess-Palaeosol formation that appears to start at 2.5Ma in China. Palaeomagnetic data indicate the lower part of the Matuyama Chron. Howvere a 1960s Uranium series date is given as 370,000 +_ 120,000 years.
primary_reference: M.V. Sotnikova, A.E. Dodonov, and AV Pen'kov. 1997. Upper Cenozoic bio-magnetic stratigraphy of Central Asian mammalian localities. Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology 133:243-258
geology_comments: The bone-bearing horizon is a 2m thick and 5m long lens within a section 35m thick. This consists of hard loess and loess-like sediments with layers of calcareous tuffs; basally the sediments lie on granite.
collection_comments: Older material is kept at the Zoological Institute of Alam-Ata (Kazakhstan). The two faunal lists are very different, only overlapping on the presence of Equus stenonis/Equus sp. (small stenonid). Both faunal lists have been entered.

lithdescript: Fossils found in reinforced red quarz sand
primary_reference: F. Heller. 1955. Ein kleiner Bar (Ursus sackdillingensis n. sp.) in der cromerischen Fauna der Sackdillinger-Hohle (Oberpfalz). Neues Jahrbuch fur Geologie und Palantologie 12:520-530
lithdescript.1: Fossils found in reinforced red quarz sand

geogcomments: Fossils are part of a museum collection and were probably found in Pikermi, which is near Euboea, the source of these coordinates
stratcomments: Dates to early Pliocene
primary_reference: J. Brunner. 1941. Ein Barenrest aus dem Unterpliozan von Euboea. Anseiger Osterreichische Akademie der Wissenschaften. Mathematisch-Naturwissenschaftliche Klasse 78(3):24-27
collection_comments: These fossils are part of a holding at the University of Athens. The author deduces that they were probably discovered at Pikermi due to sediment adherant to a skull fragment and because of the frequency of expeditions to this site.

geogcomments: 18km northeast of Baldzhuan (on which the co-ordinates are based) in the River Kuruksai Valley, Afgahn-Tadzhik Depression, southeastern Tadjikistan.
stratcomments: Middle Villafranchian, Late Pliocene, MN17. Below the Olduvai Subchron in the lower part of the Matuyama Chron. At the top of the Kuruksay/Kuruksai suite. There are 4 layers at the site - the three lowest are conglomerates and aleurites belonging to the Kuruksai suite (the bone bed is the second of these layers). The topmost bed is largely loessic clay and belongs to the Kairubak suite.
lithdescript: Fluvial deposits - siltstone, sandstone with interbedded conglomerates, belonging to the Kuruksay suite.
primary_reference: M.V. Sotnikova, A.E. Dodonov, and AV Pen'kov. 1997. Upper Cenozoic bio-magnetic stratigraphy of Central Asian mammalian localities. Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology 133:243-258
lithdescript.1: Fluvial deposits - siltstone, sandstone with interbedded conglomerates, belonging to the Kuruksay suite.
collection_comments: Two localities have been excavated - Kuruksai-Navrukho and Kuruksai-Lagernaya. This is probably the faunal list from Navrukho.\r\nThe locality was discovered in 1962.

primary_reference: M. Baudry. 1994. Les Gisements de mammifères du Miocène supérieur de Kemiklitepe, Turquie: 6. Hyracoidea. Bulletin du Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle. Section C: Sciences de la Terre: Paléontologie, Géologie, Minéralogie 16(1):113-141
taxonomy_comments:
Pliohyrax from Baudry (1994). All other taxa from:

Bouvrain, G. 1994. Les Gisements de mammifères du Miocène supérieur de Kemiklitepe, Turquie: 9. Bovidae. Bulletin du Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle. Section C: Sciences de la Terre: Paléontologie, Géologie, Minéralogie, 16(1):175-209.

geogcomments: Menacer is located at the extreme west end of the Mitidja plain, 60 kilometers west-southwest of Alger.
stratcomments: No radiometric dates available. A Turolian age (5-7 Ma) is reported in the NOW database and is based on faunal correlations.
lithdescript: Fossiliferous lignites are interstratified with continental sands and lacustrine deposits. These beds are 200-250 meters thick.
primary_reference: H. Thomas and G. Petter. 1986. Révision de la fuane de mammifères du Miocène supérieur de Menacer (ex-Marceau), Algérie: Discussion sur l'âge du gisement. Géobios 19(3):357-373
lithdescript.1: Fossiliferous lignites are interstratified with continental sands and lacustrine deposits. These beds are 200-250 meters thick.
collection_comments: Number of unidentified specimens not reported.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on Lake Koronia (also called Lake Langada), approx. 4km north of the site. In the Mygdonia basin.
stratcomments: Late Villafranchian, similar to Gerakarou 1, MNQ 18, Late Villanyian, late Pliocene\r\n
primary_reference: G. D. Koufos. 2001. The Villafranchian mammalian faunas and biochronology of Greece. Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 40(2):217-223
taxonomy_comments: only skulls and teeth included in numbers of specimens given in Koufos and Kostopoulos (1997).

geogcomments: Jiegou, Dongxiang autonomous county, Gansu province, China. Coordinates are for Linxia (NEEDS TO BE CORRECTED/REFINED).
stratcomments: "Baodean" stage
lithdescript: "red clay with calcareous nodules about 50m thick"
primary_reference: Z.-x. Qiu, J.-y. Xie, and Y. Defa. 1991. Discovery of Late Miocene Agriotherium from Jiegou, Gansu, and its taxonomic implications. Vertebrata PalAsiatica 29(4):286-295
lithdescript.1: "red clay with calcareous nodules about 50m thick"

geogcomments: The fossiliferous site Libakos is situated north of the village Polylakkos (Kozani, Macedonia, Greece) into the wider valley of the river Haliakmon (Eltgen 1968, p. 114; Steensma 1988) (Figure 1a). The mammal fossils were found in this ravine, but the exact point is not referred.
stratcomments: Combining all the biochronological data, provided from the carnivores and the chronological ones from the neighbouring Greek localities, the Libakos fauna could be dated to late Villafranchian between ~2.0 and 1.5 Ma. As the study of the Libakos fauna (bovids, cervids, equids, proboscideans) is continued, new data will allow a more precise age determination.
lithdescript: cross-bedded sands, gravels and clays
primary_reference: G. D. Koufos. 2001. The Villafranchian mammalian faunas and biochronology of Greece. Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 40(2):217-223
lithdescript.1: cross-bedded sands, gravels and clays
collection_comments: The Libakos material was collected by Eltgen during the years 1979–1982 and housed for a long time in the Institut für Geologie und Paläontologie, Technischen Universitat Clausthal, Germany. In 2014, the Laboratory of Geology and Palaeontology of the Aristotle University of Thessaloniki (LGPUT) asked from the above institute to give back the fossils and now they are stored in its collection.
taxonomy_comments: The ursid of Libakos is represented by a calcaneus and a third phalanx. Both specimens are quite larger than U. etruscus and closer in size to the younger (middle Pleistocene) U. deningeri, and thus they are referred as Ursus sp. (Koufos et al. 2018). These two specimens probably originated from a younger horizon, and they are mixed with Libakos collection, or they are coming from surface collection.

geogcomments: near the village of Nea Apollonia, co-ordinates given are based on the town of Marathousa, approx. 7km south of the site.
stratcomments: Mygdonia Basin, MNQ 20, latest (end) Villafranchian, Late Lower Pleistocene, end Biharian, approx. 1.0Ma?
primary_reference: G. D. Koufos. 2001. The Villafranchian mammalian faunas and biochronology of Greece. Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 40(2):217-223
collection_comments: Material held in the Laboratory of Geology and Palaeontology, University of Thessaloniki, Greece.
taxonomy_comments: New species of Canid named in Koufos and Kostopoulos (1997). Only skulls and teeth included in numbers of specimens given in Koufos and Kostopoulos (1997).

geogcomments: Cave site on the southern edge of the Sierra de Segura (Alfaguara). Co-ordinates based on the nearby town of Tiscar-Don Pedro.
stratcomments: Late Middle Pleistocene, similar to Arago.
primary_reference: N. L. Martinez and A. R. Bustos. 1977. Descubrimiento de dos yacimientos del Pleistoceno medio en el karst de la Sierra Alfaguara (Granada). Sintesis estratigraphica de este periodo en la region Betica. Estudios Geologicos 33:255-265

geogcomments: Co-ordinate based on the town of Tiscar-Don Pedro. In the Sierra Alfaguara (nr. Sierra de Segura). In the North east of the Granada depression.
stratcomments: Early Middle Pleistocene. similar to Mt. Peglia, Betfia 2. (pre-Cromerian).
primary_reference: N. L. Martinez and A. R. Bustos. 1977. Descubrimiento de dos yacimientos del Pleistoceno medio en el karst de la Sierra Alfaguara (Granada). Sintesis estratigraphica de este periodo en la region Betica. Estudios Geologicos 33:255-265

geogcomments: sites are in the Sierra de Atapuerca, 14km east of the city of Burgos, Northern Spain.
stratcomments: TD6 dated between 0.99Ma (Jaramillo) and the Brunhes/Matuyama boundary (0.78Ma), uppermost level of TD6 is the Aurora Stratum, containing Homo antecessor remains). Plans and maps given in Carbonell et al. (1999).
lithdescript: Gravel and boulders
primary_reference: N. Garcia and J. L. Arsuaga. 1999. Carnivores from the Early Pleistocene hominid-bearing Trinchera Dolina 6 (Sierra de Atapuerca, Spain). Journal of Human Evolution 37:415-430
lithdescript.1: Gravel and boulders
collection_comments: Material currently held in the Museo Nacional de Ciencias Naturales, Madrid and Laboratori de Arqeologia de la Universitat Rovira i Virgili, Tarragona. They will be permanently stored in the Museo de Burgos.
taxonomy_comments: This list includes all animals reported from TD6 (except the carnivore list which has been subdivided). See Atapuerca Aurora stratum record for those reported just from the hominid layer.

geogcomments: Sites are in the Sierra de Atapuerca, 14 km east of the city of Burgos, Northern Spain.
stratcomments: This is the hominid bearing Aurora stratum, which is the top section of TD6, between T36-43. Gran Dolina is formed of 11 levels. Dated between 0.99Ma (Jaramillo) and the Brunhes/Matuyama boundary (0.78Ma).
lithdescript: 25cm thick layer with a silty matrix and decimetric clasts.
primary_reference: N. Garcia and J. L. Arsuaga. 1999. Carnivores from the Early Pleistocene hominid-bearing Trinchera Dolina 6 (Sierra de Atapuerca, Spain). Journal of Human Evolution 37:415-430
lithdescript.1: 25cm thick layer with a silty matrix and decimetric clasts.
collection_comments: Material currently held in the Museo Nacional de Ciencias Naturales, Madrid and Laboratori de Arqeologia de la Universitat Rovira i Virgili, Tarragona. They will be permanently stored in the Museo de Burgos.
taxonomy_comments: This is the list for the Aurora stratum, where this distinction has been made in the publications. The birds (Aves) hae not been divided and have not been included in this list. For a full list of birds in the whole level see the record for Atapuerca TD6. Ungulate identifications in Diez et al. (1999) do not correspond with those in van der Made (1999).

geogcomments: co-ordinates based on the town of Orce, which is slightly to the south west of the site.
stratcomments: Belonging to Guadix-Baza basin. dated to 1.2+-0.2Ma. Lower Pleistocene. According to Augustì et al. (2010) 1.6-1.4Ma.
lithdescript: 'white carbonate facies (mudstone) with palaeosols, carbonate concretions and abundant skeletal remains of fossil mammals'. Forms a layer 2.5km long. Venta Micena stratum is 80-120cm thick. 'Previous studies indicate that the Venta Micena assemblage is included in 90-98% pure micritic limestone, which precipitated in freshwater ponds emplaced on a caliche paleosol of diagenetic origin'
primary_reference: B. Martinez-Navarro. 1992. Revision sistematica de la fauna de macromamiferos del yacimiento de Venta Micena (Orce, Granada, Espana). in Gibert, J. ed., Proyecto Orce-Cueva Victoria (1988-1992): Presencia humana en el Pleistoceno inferior de Granada y Murcia. Ayuntamiento de Orce, Granada, Spain.
lithdescript.1: 'white carbonate facies (mudstone) with palaeosols, carbonate concretions and abundant skeletal remains of fossil mammals'. Forms a layer 2.5km long. Venta Micena stratum is 80-120cm thick. 'Previous studies indicate that the Venta Micena assemblage is included in 90-98% pure micritic limestone, which precipitated in freshwater ponds emplaced on a caliche paleosol of diagenetic origin'
geology_comments: The palaeosol containing the Venta Micena assemblage 'surrounded a more or less stable and shallow lake with swampy margin zones that existed in the valley of Orce during the Plio-Pleistocene'. The Mudstone (see lithology) is interpreted as a 'lake with various periods of partial dessication, during one of which the Venta Micena assemblage was formed'.

geogcomments: In the Sierra de Atapuerca, 14km east of Burgos, Northern Spain
stratcomments: level TD4 has been dated after the Jaramillo event (0.99Ma) and before the Brunhes/Matuyama boundary (0.78) and is therefore late Lower Pleistocene.
primary_reference: N. Garcia and J. L. Arsuaga. 1999. Carnivores from the Early Pleistocene hominid-bearing Trinchera Dolina 6 (Sierra de Atapuerca, Spain). Journal of Human Evolution 37:415-430
collection_comments: 'on the basis of pollen analysis, a forested landscape dominated TD4 times, with Quercus and Pinus. The presence of leafy deciduous trees (Fagus, Betula, Juglans, Ulmus) and Olea indicate a transition between cold and warm conditions.'

geogcomments: In the Sierra de Atapuerca, 14km east of the city of Burgos, Northern Spain.
stratcomments: Dated between 0.99Ma (end Jaramillo) and 0.78Ma (Brunhes/Matuyama boundary).

geogcomments: Sites are in the Sierra de Atapuerca, 14km east of the city of Burgos, Northern Spain

geogcomments: co-ordinates based on the town of Orce, some 5km to the west.
stratcomments: Lower Pleistocene, in the Matuyama Chron (1.77-0.78Ma), possibly predates the Jaramillo event (1.07-0.99Ma), Upper Biharian. Out of a total of 15 levels, 4 archaeological and palaeontological levels are recognised, in a variety of depositional environments, but only one faunal list is given.
lithdescript: 'the locality under study is found in carbonatic sediments of the section. In this area it was possible to conduct a precise geological study of the sedimentary cycle of the paleo-lake of Orce to a depth of some 5m. From one upper limestone level to another lower limestone and marl level, six geological units have been identified: I) assemblage of limestones and marls; II) carbonated clays; III) clays and silts; IV) carbonated clays and marls; V) green clays oxidised at their summit; VI) marly limestones. Archaeological level 1 is in geological unit II, archaeological level 2 in geological unit V and archaeological level 3 in geological unit VI.
primary_reference: T. Medin, B. Martinez-Navarro, and F. Rivals, J. Madurell-Malapeira, S. Ros-Montoya, M.-P. Espigares, B. Figueirido, L. Rook, P. Palmqvist. 2017. Late Villafranchian Ursus etruscus and other large carnivorans from the Orce sites (Guadix-Baza basin, Andalusia, southern Spain): Taxonomy, biochronology, paleobiology, and ecogeographical context. Quaternary International 431:20-41
lithdescript.1: 'the locality under study is found in carbonatic sediments of the section. In this area it was possible to conduct a precise geological study of the sedimentary cycle of the paleo-lake of Orce to a depth of some 5m. From one upper limestone level to another lower limestone and marl level, six geological units have been identified: I) assemblage of limestones and marls; II) carbonated clays; III) clays and silts; IV) carbonated clays and marls; V) green clays oxidised at their summit; VI) marly limestones. Archaeological level 1 is in geological unit II, archaeological level 2 in geological unit V and archaeological level 3 in geological unit VI.
collection_comments: Found on May 5th 1991, Excavated in April 1994 and August 1995.
taxonomy_comments: degree of evolution of Allophaiomys burgondiae and A. chalinei suggest that FN3 is older than the Gran Dolina, Atapuerca.

geogcomments: in the lower Elwy valley of North Wales, approx. 4km west of Trefnant
stratcomments: hominid from the Intermediate Complex, all others from both the Intermediate Complex and the Lower Breccia. Dating done with Uranium series, and thermoluminescence yield dates 200-180kyr
lithdescript: Intermediate Complex are transitional between sand/gravel units and breccia units. Unit is characterized by the presence of siliceous pebbles, badly sorted coarse sand and highly altered limestone clasts.\r\nBreccia units comprise many angular coarse particles, mainly limestone, with medium to strong carbonate cement.
primary_reference: H. S. Green, C. B. Stringer, and S. N. Collcutt, A. P. Currant, J. Huxtable, H. P. Schwarcz, N. Debenham, C. Embleton, P. Bull, T. I. Molleson, R. E. Bevins. 1981. Pontnewydd Cave in Wales -- a new Middle Pleistocene hominid site. Nature 294(24):707-713
lithdescript.1: Intermediate Complex are transitional between sand/gravel units and breccia units. Unit is characterized by the presence of siliceous pebbles, badly sorted coarse sand and highly altered limestone clasts.\r\nBreccia units comprise many angular coarse particles, mainly limestone, with medium to strong carbonate cement.
geology_comments: fluvial and mudflow deposits into a cave
collection_comments: also includes archaeological material (300 artefacts)

stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene

geogcomments: Osztramos Hill 'rises insularly above the basin of the Upper Bodva between Tornaszentandras and Bodvarako, as the northernmost member of the Rudabanya mountains (in the very north of Hungary).'
stratcomments: Middle/Late Lower Pleistocene, similar age to Locality 8, between 1.5 and 1.0Ma.\r\nLocality 2 consisted of the filling of an enormous cave in the eastern portion of section XII of the quarry, 320 a.s.l. 'The profile of cave, filled entirely with red clay, was 20m long by 14m high at discovery. About 2.5-3.5m above the basement, two layers sloping towards the north were found to contain extreme accumulations of bones which we fully exploited and sedimented.'
lithdescript: 'red clay.'
primary_reference: D. Janossy. 1986. Pleistocene vertebrate faunas of Hungary. Developments in Palaeontology and Stratigraphy, 8. Elsevier, Amsterdam
lithdescript.1: 'red clay.'
collection_comments: The cave in question was opened in 1965 and totally blasted in 1969.
taxonomy_comments: The faunal list is from the lower layer, the few large mammal finds were from mixed, waste rock material.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the town of Nagyharsany. 'parallel to the the main range of the Villany mountains, some 2 km distance to the south, is the 3km long, narrow Nagyharsanyhegy 442m a.s.l., with soaring steep ledges to the south and north.'
stratcomments: Biharian, Late Lower Pleistocene. 'Kretzoi employed the designation no. 4 for those sites in the eastern wall of the eastern quarry of the hill which Kormos described as hollows filled with brown sediment.'
lithdescript: 'hollows filled with brown sediment'
primary_reference: D. Janossy. 1986. Pleistocene vertebrate faunas of Hungary. Developments in Palaeontology and Stratigraphy, 8. Elsevier, Amsterdam
lithdescript.1: 'hollows filled with brown sediment'
geology_comments: 'The southern slope and the ridge are very strongly karstic, and the hill is composed of very steep, in places almost vertical Mesozoic formations. The first of these are Anisian dolomite in the north, which is followed by Callovian Cornbrash limestone (according to recent studies: Pliensbachian (Lower Jurassic) and then Callovian layers), then Lower and Upper Malm limestone, then a sediment hiatus in which a bauxite formation is intercalated; finally, Lower Cretaceous rocks on the southern slope of the hill. Here and there red clay containing bone remains fills the vertical clefs of the system of faults marring the hill.' (originally in German in Kretzoi (1956) translated into English by Janossy (1986).
collection_comments: 'the literature contains a series of mistakes concerning this locality, and some of the previous lists certainly do not represent similar aged material.' The locality was subsequently demolished by quarrying.
taxonomy_comments: This list 'incorporates corrections made by Kormos and Kretzoi.'

geogcomments: 'a small unused quarry located in the vicinity of Repashuta (on which the co-ordinates are based) in the Bukk mountains, on a ridge between Kovesvarad and Zsindelyesteto, about 50m south-southwest of the highway between Eger and Lillafured.'

geology_comments: 'A karstic pit about 5m high and 2-3m wide was found in compact Triassic Ladinian limestone in the northern corner of the quarry. This was filled with orange sediment .... washed almost in its entirety in 1957-58.'
collection_comments: Discovered by Janossy in 1955. Different strata were not identified.\r\n
taxonomy_comments: forest environment.

stratcomments: Three levels with fauna recorded, the lower gravels (unteren Kiesen), The loam zone (Lehmzone), and the upper gravels (Oberen Kiesen). The lower gravels are the lowest level and represent a cold period. The Middle level (Lehmzone) is a warm period = Early Middle Pleistocene, the Voigtstedt-Warmzeit, definitely the Brunhes Chron.
primary_reference: W. von Koenigswald and W.-D. Heinrich. 1999. Mittelpleistozane Saugetierfaunen aus Mitteleuropa - der Versuch einer biostratigraphischen Zuordnung. Kaupia 9:53-112
collection_comments: The Loam zone contains the most species and represents a warm period (the Voigtstedt-Warmzeit).

geogcomments:
There are a lot of Tongshan (copper mountains) in Anhui province, and in the article there is no specific city or county mentioned, so the coordinates I took are without any certainty; an other possibility is: 30°27'21''N and 117°16'38''E, but I prefere the first one, because it is nearer to the Changjiang river. But maybe it is another Tongshan...
Describtion from the text: 
China, Anhui province, Wannan prefecture (Wannan means southern Anhui), Tongshan (copper mountain); Tongshan is about 15 km to the south of Changjiang (Yangzekiang) river; the fossil locality of Tongshan is a cave near the northern wall of a cement factory.
stratcomments: late Middle Pleistocene to Upper Pleistocene.\nbed 3 in the lcoal section\nThe wall of the cave has long ago broken down, so that a detailed investigation is not possible.
lithdescript: Brown-red sandy clay, intercalated with sand and pebbles, occasionally some big calcareous pebbles; altogether 2,5-3 m thick.
primary_reference: Y. Ye and D. Yan. 1975. A new locality of Quaternary mammalian fossils at Tongshan in the south of Anhui Province. Vertebrata PalAsiatica (=Gujizhui dongwu yu gurenlei) 13(3):195-196
lithdescript.1: Brown-red sandy clay, intercalated with sand and pebbles, occasionally some big calcareous pebbles; altogether 2,5-3 m thick.
geology_comments: It is not quite clear if the fossil locality is a cave or a fissure.

geogcomments: Co-ordinate based on the nearest village which is Kibbutz Beit Zera. Site is south of Lake Galilee (= Lake Kinneret), in the Jordan Valley.
stratcomments: Site has been subject to much tectonic movement - archaeological levels are almost vertical in the section. There have been four major depositional cycles, the lowest is of archaeological interest and is termed the Fi (lower Fluviatile). This limnic level is 30m thick. Site dated to between 1.4-1.0Ma, but is thought (on faunal grounds) to be at the older end of this range.
lithdescript: 'The Fi cycle consists of clay, clastic conglomerates, chalky marls, silt and basaltic sands, often altered by paleosols.' Site II-23 is a paleosol, comprising a silty clay lens, c.20 cm thick.
primary_reference: S. Gaudzinski. 2004. Subsistence patterns of Early Pleistocene hominids in the Levant - taphonomic evidence from the 'Ubeidiya Formation. Journal of Archaeological Science 31:65-75
lithdescript.1: 'The Fi cycle consists of clay, clastic conglomerates, chalky marls, silt and basaltic sands, often altered by paleosols.' Site II-23 is a paleosol, comprising a silty clay lens, c.20 cm thick.
geology_comments: The site is in the northern end of the Red Sea-East African Rift. 'The Formation represents a tectonic anticline with undulations and faults. Sediments are tilted up to 80 degrees and are exposed over an areas of approximately 1km2'. Local environment - 'the delta of an ephemeral stream debouched into a freshwater lake.'
collection_comments: Level II-23 was excavated between 1961-63. 105 m2 were exposed. 33 artifacts were recovered and 2 cutmarked bones, plus 12 specimens with conical impacts which could be hominid, carnivore or trampling damage. The cutmarks were found on a cervid distal humerus and a horse mandible fragment.
taxonomy_comments: 22 hyaena coprolites (i.d. as Crocuta crocuta) were also present, although they are of differing sizes (adult vs. juvenile?). Possibly part of the II-23 assemblage comes from an hyaena den, although no bones of the animals themselves were found.

geogcomments: 'nearly directly south and 5.5km distant from the eponymous village. The village is 17km north-northwest of Ilgin (1030m) (on which the co-ordinates are based), and Ilgin is 54km northwest of Konya town in southern Anatolia.'
stratcomments: The Dursunlu deposit comprises (lower) Pleistocene lacustrine limestones, marls and clays with lignite beds. Cores suggest some 45-50m of sedimentary infill.\r\nThe basal portion of core one is +5m and is fine reddish mud, presumed alluvial with a terrestrial origin.The overlying 45m are a 'shallowing upwards lacustrine sequence, incorporating intermittent episodes of fluctuating lake level(s), shallowing and even subaerial exposure(s).'\r\nDating - palaeomagentism indicates Reversed polarity with two weak Normal records. It is suggested that this represents the lower part of the Matuyama Chron. Biharian on faunal records.
lithdescript: Vertebrate fossils are confirmed in two levels of lignite, but others may have come from elsewhere. The known fossiliferous layers are in the upper lignite member at -10 to -12m and in the basal part of bedded silty clays at -4m. \r\nThe majority of the cores are greenish, partially laminated clays, with lignite bands and occasional silty clays with rootlets and molluscs.
primary_reference: E. Gulec, F. C. Howell, and T. D. White. 1999. Dursunlu - a new Lower Pleistocene faunal and artifact-bearing locality in southern Anatolia. in Ullrich, H., ed, Hominid evolution - Lifestyles and survival strategies. Edition Archaea, Gelsenkirchen/Schwelm.
lithdescript.1: Vertebrate fossils are confirmed in two levels of lignite, but others may have come from elsewhere. The known fossiliferous layers are in the upper lignite member at -10 to -12m and in the basal part of bedded silty clays at -4m. \r\nThe majority of the cores are greenish, partially laminated clays, with lignite bands and occasional silty clays with rootlets and molluscs.
collection_comments:
Fossils and artefacts were recovered from the spoil heaps of a large lignite quarry.
Microfaunal remains were recovered by sieving.
Collection contains 175 archaeoogical artefacts.
Specimens kept at MTA, Ankara.
taxonomy_comments: Also 3 larger undetermined bovid specimens, represented by 2 lower teeth and a tarsal (cuboravicular).\r\n41 species of birds have also been identified, but these were reported elsewhere.

collection_name: Valea Graunceanului, Tetoiu 1 lower faunal horizon
geogcomments: Between Tetoiu (on which co-ordinates are based) and Irimesti (9km apart), in the Oltet river valley, in a valley called Valea Graunceanului (also called Valea Tetoi), on the western slope of a hill named Dealul Viilor. 2.5km north of La Pietris.
stratcomments: Late Pliocene, just before the Olduvai event (based on palaeomagnetism). Faunal comparison indicates that the Lower Faunal Horizon (Tetoiu 1) correlates with the upper part of the Khaprov faunal complex, upper MN17 (St Vallier Zone), probably equivalent to the Tiglian warm stage. It is suggested that Valea Graunceanului correlates with zone 5C of the Tiglian, and is a warmer pahse than that represnted by La Pietris.\r\nSite is approximately 88m below the top of the section, at the base of a silty sand, about 1.5m thick.
primary_reference: A. Bolomey. 1965. Die fauna zweier villafrankischer Fundstellen in Rumaenien. Berichte der geologischen Gesellschaft der DDR, Berlin 10(1):77-88
geology_comments: The lower faunal horizon is in an approximately 20m thick bed consisting 'predominantly of silty sands and sand, levels with gravels and larger pebbles were also observed. These beds are rich in faunal remains.' 'The beds have an average dip of 1 to 2 degrees SE.'\r\n

lithdescript: Karst infilling, with frequent occurrence of quartz grains - probably derived from the Apennines - indicating a palaeogeography similar to that of today.
primary_reference: C. De Guili, F. Masini, and D. Torre. 1990. The Latest Villfranchian faunas in Italy - the Pirro Nord local fauna (Apricena, Gargano). Quatarpalaontologie 8:29-34

collection_comments: Material collected in 1978.

geogcomments: Located about 9 km north of Lufeng, at the southern side of Miaoshanpo-Mountain; IVPP-Point 75033, called section D.
stratcomments: Quarry sequence is divided into 8 beds (numbered from top), in the Xi River Basin north of Lufeng, China. These unconformably overly Mesozoic sediments. No Formation names are given.\r\nHan 1985: "Its geological age is Late Miocene, equivalent to the Turolian of European sequence of land mammal ages." Qi 1986: Age "8 Ma or perhaps a bit later"
lithdescript: Quarry section at Lufeng is 8 m thick. Detailed microstratigraphy given in Qi 1985 and Badgley et al 1988.
primary_reference: G. Qi. 1985. A preliminary report on Carnivora from the Ramapithecus locality, Lufeng, Yunnan. Acta Anthropologica Sinica (Renleixue xuebao) 4(1):33-43
lithdescript.1: Quarry section at Lufeng is 8 m thick. Detailed microstratigraphy given in Qi 1985 and Badgley et al 1988.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Xiaodian, the locality is near here.
stratcomments: Early Middle Miocene, Orleanian, MN 5, Shanwangian.
primary_reference: Z. Qiu. 1990. The Chinese Neogene mammalian biochronology - its correlation with the European Neogene mammal zonation. In E.H. Lindsay, V. Fahlbusch and P. Mein, eds., European Neogene Mammal Chronology. Plenum Press, New York.
taxonomy_comments: The identification of Macaca is controversial, could be a suid.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the city of Opole in Southwestern Poland, the site is approximately 15km Southeast of the city.
stratcomments: Middle Miocene, MN 5-7.
lithdescript: 'contained bones of vertebrates and shells of snails in loams filling a channel and covered by lignites.'
primary_reference: K. Kowalski. 1990. Stratigraphy of Neogene mammals in Poland. In E.H. Lindsay, V. Fahlbusch and P. Mein, eds., European Neogene Mammal Chronology, Plenum Press, New York
lithdescript.1: 'contained bones of vertebrates and shells of snails in loams filling a channel and covered by lignites.'
collection_comments: Fauna needs revising, Opole 1 was discovered and published by Wagner in 1913, but most of the specimens have disappeared since then.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Blassac, approximately 800m from the site. The site is visible from the road RN 585 from Lavoute-Chilhac to Brioude.
stratcomments: Early Pleistocene, based on fauna, but the overlying basalt has been dated at 2Ma.
lithdescript: alluvium.
primary_reference: E. Heintz, C. Guerin, and R. Martin, F. Prat. 1974. Principaux gisements villafranchiens de France: Listes fauniques et biostratigraphie. Memoires du Bureau de Recherches Geologiques et Minieres  78(1):169-182
lithdescript.1: alluvium.
geology_comments: a lacustrine delta.
collection_comments: Excavated in 1965-67, 1971-73. All specimens held in the Laboratoire de Paleontologie des Vertebres et Paleontologie Humaine, Faculte des Sciences, Universite de Poitiers.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the Nestore River, near Perugia. The site is a lignite mine to the south of Lake Trasimento, in the Tavernelle Basin.
stratcomments: Early Pleistocene, Early Biharian, Farneta faunal unit, Latest Villafranchian. There are three separate lignite mine workings, but the levels are thought to be the roughly the same date based on the macro- and micromammalian remains.
primary_reference: S. Gentili, L. Abbazzi, and F. Masini, P. Ambrosetti, P. Argenti, D. Torre. 1996. Voles from the Early Pleistocene of Pietrafitta (central Italy, Perugia). Acta Zoologica Cracovensia 39(1):185-199
geology_comments: swampy environment.
collection_comments: The specimens were collcetd from an active lignite mine, as and when they were found. The material is stored in a small museum on the site.
taxonomy_comments: The dominance of arvicolids nay suggest an environment chracaterised locally by open areas with a cold climate. This hypothesis is further strengthened by the presence of the giant deer Megaceroides obscurus. Mimomys savini has been reported previously, but this is thought to have been the mis-identification of a large Mimomys chalinei upper M1.

geogcomments: Site is 22km west of Istanbul, on which the co-ordinates are based. It is on the east side of the lower Salizdere valley, which drains into the Kucukcekmece Lagoon, and then into the sea of Marmara.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene, probably Late Middle Pleistocene. The cave is 600m deep, with 2 entrances. Deposits within 70m of the lower entrance contain animal remains and palaeolithic artefacts.
primary_reference: M. C. Stiner, G. Arsebuk, and F. C. Howell. 1996. Cave bears and paleolithic artifacts in Yarimburgaz Cave, Turkey: Dissecting a palimpsest. Geoarchaeology 11(4):279-327
geology_comments: 'part of an extensive karst system in Eocene Limestone.'
collection_comments: Middle Pleistocene levels excavated from 1988-1990, by the University of Istanbul and University of California at Berkeley. Material stored at the Section of Prehistory, University of Istanbul.
taxonomy_comments: The majority of remains are cave bears (93%), 4% ungulates (151 specimens) and 3% carnivores (NISP = 109). Faunal list is broken down into trenches and geological areas, but only genera/size classes are given, whereas the full faunal list has specific level destinctions. The complete faunal list for all trenches has been entered here. This paper only lists large mammal remains, hare is also mentioned, so this may not be the full list.

geogcomments: The site is on the East side of Capri Island, some 3km from Sorrento (on mainland Italy).
stratcomments: Late Middle Pleistocene, based on fauna. There are two localites (Quisisana and Certosa) in a south sloping terrace and several collections dating back to 1901 are included in this faunal list. They have all been referred to 'Quisisana-Certosa'. The site has been controversial because of the possible association of stone tools with Middle Pleistocene faunas.
lithdescript: The lithology has been descibed by several different authors as 'lacustrine oxidised clays, with vertebrate bones and chert artefacts.', 'Red clays dipping west without volcanic minerals and with vertebrate bones. The upper portion of this level bears lithic artefacts.' and 'Red clays (= 'Lehm' Auct.) with an acheulean industry in the upper part, overlying Middle Pleistocene vertebrate remains' (Other layers with Late Pleistocene remains overlie this).
primary_reference: L. Capasso Barbato and E. Gliozzi. 1995. Biochronological and palaeogeographical implications of a well-balanced late Middle Pleistocene fauna from Quisiana-Certosa (Capri, Southern Italy). Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 34(2):235-261
lithdescript.1: The lithology has been descibed by several different authors as 'lacustrine oxidised clays, with vertebrate bones and chert artefacts.', 'Red clays dipping west without volcanic minerals and with vertebrate bones. The upper portion of this level bears lithic artefacts.' and 'Red clays (= 'Lehm' Auct.) with an acheulean industry in the upper part, overlying Middle Pleistocene vertebrate remains' (Other layers with Late Pleistocene remains overlie this).
collection_comments: Fossils stored at the Museum of the Sezione Caprense 'Ignazio Cerio' of the Instituto Italiano di Palaeontologia Umana, in Museo di Paleontologia, University of Naples 'Frederico II' and Instituto Italiano di Palaeontologia Umana, Rome.

collection_name: Artenac 'Premiere macromammal Assemblage', Commune de Saint-Mary, Charente,
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the city of Angouleme, the site is 20km North East of the city on a tributary of the Charente called the Tardoire.
stratcomments: There are five members at this site ranging in age from the Middle to Upper Pleistocene. Members 1-3 (beds I-V) are Middle Pleistocene levels (estimated at 0.7-0.5Ma = 'Mindel'), The Upper Pleistocene is represented by Members 4-5 (beds 5-10). The macromammal fauna is subdivided into groups 1-3 and these are entered here.
lithdescript: beds 1b-2 are clay or clayey beds, whilst bed 3 is a thin layer of breccia.
primary_reference: A. Delagnes, J.-F. Tournepiche, and D. Armand, E. Desclaux, M.-F. Diot, C. Ferrier, V. Le Fillatre, B. Vandermeersch. 1999. Le gisement Pleistocene moyen et superieur d'Artenac (Saint-Mary, Charent): premier bilan interdisciplinaire. Bulletin de la Societe Prehistorique Francaise 96(4):469-496
lithdescript.1: beds 1b-2 are clay or clayey beds, whilst bed 3 is a thin layer of breccia.
collection_comments: This material is still under study.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the town of Soave between Verona and Vicenza. Site is on Monte Tondo nearby.

primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231

collection_name: Grotta del Cere, Verona, Northern Italy
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Boscochiesanuova. Site is marked in the same place as the site Boscochiesanuova on the map (page 186). Site is actually in the Vajo dei Falconi, near Ceredo (S. Anna d'Alfaedo), some 25km north of Verona.
stratcomments: The date of the site has caused some controversy as there is a mixture of material, some specimens date to the Penulitimate Interglacial (OIS 5, Ipswichian in UK sequence), some may be a little older.

collection_comments: Material curated in the Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Verona.

collection_name: Serbaro di Romagnano, Commune di Grezzana, Verona, Northern Italy
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Romagnano, the site is near here.
stratcomments: The material has been looked at briefly by a number of authors, but the assemblage was not systematically collected and therefore a secure date cannot be applied, other than stating that it is a Middle Pleistocene assemblage.
lithdescript: ossiferous breccia.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
lithdescript.1: ossiferous breccia.
geology_comments: The site is a 'series of ossiferous berccias in a split' (probably a fissure fill).
collection_comments: The majority of the material is curated in the Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Verona.

collection_name: Fornace di Cornedo (Pleistocene), Cornedo Vincentino, Valle dell'Agno, Vicenza
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Cornedo Vincentino. The site is near here in the Valle dell'Agno.
stratcomments: Mixed fauna, some is Holocene (entered separately). ?Late Middle Pleistocene.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
geology_comments: 'Il giacimento e noto dalla fine del secolo scorso.'
collection_comments: The fauna is fairly heterogeneous, and mixed. The specimens have not always been collected according to correct methods. Specimens are in a variety of collections, most are in the Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Verona and the Museo di Paleontologia dell'Univesita di Padova.

collection_name: Fornace di Cornedo (Pleistocene), Cornedo Vincentino, Valle dell'Agno, Vicenza

primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
geology_comments: 'Il giacimento e noto dalla fine del secolo scorso.'

collection_name: Grotta Maggiore do S. Bernardino (Pleistocene), Colli Berici, Northern Italy
geogcomments: Site is on the slopes of Colli Berici, close to Palu di Mossano, approximately 20km south of Vicenza city (on which the co-ordinates are based).
stratcomments: Late Pleistocene. The site is a large cave. Much sediment was eroded or damaged when the site was lived in, in the Medieval period. The date of the site is in dispute, some authors say the last glacial (Devensian in the UK sequence), others say there is a Holocene element. The Holocene specimens include domestic species and these have been entered separately. The material was first published by Fabiani 1903 and 1919, and he included both Pleistocene and Holocene faunas in those lists.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
collection_comments: The material is held in The Departimento di Scienze Geologiche e Paleontologiche dell'Universita di Ferrara and the Museo Civico di Vicenza.
taxonomy_comments: The material reported to be Holocene includes domestic species and has been entered separately.

collection_name: Spessa 2, Colli Berici, Vincenza, Northern Italy
geogcomments: The site is on the extreme southern edge of Colle Berici, near the village of Spessa (on which the co-ordinates are based).
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene.
lithdescript: bone breccia.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
lithdescript.1: bone breccia.
collection_comments: The material is held in the Departimento di Scienze Geologiche e Paleontologiche dell'Universita di Ferrara.

geogcomments: A cave in the comune di Sgonico on which the co-ordinates are based. Near Trieste.
stratcomments: Early Middle Pleistocene, Galerian.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
geology_comments: Infilling a space in a cave of onyx. 'un riempimento carsico posto in una parete a picco in una cava di onice.'
collection_comments: The site was very rich in fossils but it has been looted and damaged during work in the cave. The material is stored in the Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Trieste.

collection_name: Grotta san Leonardo, Sgonico, Trieste, Northern Italy.
geogcomments: On the south site of the eponymous mountain (Monte San Leonardo?), in the comune di Sgonico, near Trieste.
stratcomments: latest Middle Pleistocene?
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
geology_comments: A cave with many stalagmites.
collection_comments: The site has been the subject of systematic excavations, yielding macro and micromammals and mousterien tools. The material is held in the Departimento di Scienze Geologiche e Paleontologiche dell'Universita di Ferrara and the Dipartimento di Scienze Archeologiche di Pisa.

collection_name: Grotta Tilde, Gabrovizza, Trieste, Northern Italy
geogcomments: In a the Grotta Tilde, near Gabrovizza on which the co-ordinates are based.
stratcomments: Middle-Upper Pleistocene.
lithdescript: bone breccia.
primary_reference: M. Bon, G. Piccoli, and B. Sala. 1991. I giacimenti Quaternari di vertebrati fossili nell'Italia Nord-Orientale. Memorie degli Instituti di Geologia e Mineralogia dell'Universita di Padova 47:185-231
lithdescript.1: bone breccia.
geology_comments: A vertical chimney within a branch of the Grotta Tilde.
collection_comments: Site found in 1896. Material stored in the Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Trieste.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the nearby town of Lecce (Maglie).
stratcomments: Riss-Wurm, or Wurm 1 - Wurm 2.

lithdescript.1: Red silt (terra rossa).

collection_name: Isernia la Pineta sector 1, Upper Voluturno, Molise
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the nearby city of Isernia in the Upper Volturno Basin.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene.
primary_reference: M. Coltorti, M. Cremaschi, and M. C. Delitala, D. Esu, M. Fornaseri, A. McPherron, M. Nicoletti, R. van Otterloo, C. Peretto, B. Sala, V. Schmidt, J. Sevink. 1982. Reversed magnetic polarity at an early Lower Pleistocene site in Central Italy. Nature 300:173-176
collection_comments: Site found 4m below the surface during road building. Small mammal remains recovered by sieving.

collection_name: Casa Frata, Arezzo, Upper Valdarno, Tuscany
geogcomments: In the Upper Valdarno, near Matassino (on which the co-ordinates are based). Not far north west of Terranova Bracciolini.
stratcomments: Upper Villafranchian, Tasso faunal unit, Early Pleistocene.
primary_reference: V. Borselli, C. De Guili, and G. Ficcarelli, M. Mazzini. 1980. Casa Frata: una localita fossilifera del Villafranchiano Superiore presso Terranuova Bracciolini (Arezzo) nel Valdarno Superiore. Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 19(2):254-258
geology_comments: sandy.
collection_comments: collection held in the Museum of Geology and Palaeontology, University of Florence.

stratcomments: Lower Pleistocene; \nAccording to the fauna the deposition was during the late phase of an interglacial. After Guenther 1975 it is Eem-interglacial or the beginning of the following Weichsel-glaciation.\nKahlke 1975: Unterer Travertin-Pariser Horizont is the border between interglacial and the beginning of the new glaciation.

geogcomments: This collection is from an open cast mine north of Duren in western Germany
stratcomments:
biozone MN 5, in the middle part of the main lignite seam, later authors list horizon as 6Ca/b
JA: "6C" entered as local bed, with no local section name

Hambach 6C local fauna can be correlated with the upper part of mammalian Neogene unit MN 5, indicating an absolute age-range of 16.0–15.2 Ma for this late Orleanian, early middle Miocene fauna (Mörs et al. 2000; Mo€rs 2002; Mörs & Kalthoff 2004).
primary_reference: T. Mors, F. Hocht, and B. Wutzler. 2000. Die erst Wirbeltierfauna aus der miozanen Braunkohle der Niederrheinischen Bucht (Ville-Schichten, Tagebau Hambach) [The first vertebrate fauna from the Miocene Ville Series of the Lower Rhine Embayment (Hambach open cast mine, western Germany)]. Paläontologische Zeitschrift 74(1/2):145-170
geology_comments: sediments are channel-fill, but environment interpreted as including brackish-estuarine, lacustrine-fluviatile and terrestrial environments\r\n\r\nchannel fill and floodplain deposits (horizon 6C according to the local lithostratigraphy) within the middle Miocene Frimmersdorf lignite seam (Mo€rs et al. 2000; Sch€afer et al. 2004).
collection_comments: this collection almost certainly comes from the same unit and same general area as Tiffney's collections Hambach 8251-13349\r\n\r\nMacaluso et al. 2021: The new taxon is described based on material stored in the Steinmann Institute of the Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn, Germany.

geogcomments: Co-ordinate based on the village of Vialette near Le Puy en Velay.
stratcomments: Villanyian, Villafranchian.
primary_reference: F. Lacombat, L. Abbazzi, and M. P. Ferretti, B. Martinez-Navarro, P.-E. Moulle, M.-R. Palombo, L. Rook, A. Turner, A. M.-F. Valli. 2008. New data on the Early Villafranchian fauna from Vialette (Haute-Loire, France) based on the collection of the Crozatier Museum (Le Puy-en-Velay, Haute-Loire, France). Quaternary International 179:64-71
collection_comments: Tapir remains are held in Paris, Lyon and Basle, etc. Rhinoceros specimens held in Basle, Musee Crozatier in Puy-en-Velay, MNHN, Paris and Lyon.
taxonomy_comments: This paper contains a revised faunal list. Where the information is available the previous identification of the specimens has also been entered.\r\nEarlier reports of Equus stenonis or Hipparion from the site are likely to be due to poorly provenanced specimens in museum collections, later excavations have not recovered any specimens and the authors therefore did not include these identfications in this list.\r\nFour specimens in Bale Museum are marked as bovid, (2 phalanges, a calcaneum and a maxilla fragemnt) but are again poorly provenanced.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on Puy de Dome near Clermont Ferrand.
stratcomments: Middle Pliocene, Villafranchian, Villanyian. Estimated to date between 2.6-2.25Ma.
primary_reference: E. Heintz, C. Guerin, and R. Martin, F. Prat. 1974. Principaux gisements villafranchiens de France: Listes fauniques et biostratigraphie. Memoires du Bureau de Recherches Geologiques et Minieres  78(1):169-182
collection_comments: This paper gives an updated faunal list. Where details have been provided on earlier identifications these have been entered too.\r\nType locailty for Sus arvernensis Croizet & Jobert, 1828, Gazella borbonica Deperet, 1884 and Pliotragus ardeus (Deperet, 1884).
taxonomy_comments: Like Vialette, the equids reported from this site appear to have been mis-identifed or poorly provenanced. Therefore the authors conclude that Equus stenonis was not present in this fauna. Hippopotamus has also been reported and subsequently discounted.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Pardines.
stratcomments: Late Pliocene, Villanyian, Villafranchian
primary_reference: E. Heintz, C. Guerin, and R. Martin, F. Prat. 1974. Principaux gisements villafranchiens de France: Listes fauniques et biostratigraphie. Memoires du Bureau de Recherches Geologiques et Minieres  78(1):169-182
taxonomy_comments: Many equid teeth and bones in Bale Museum have stenonine characteristics.

geogcomments: One km from Buñol, near the highway from Yatova, and in the vicinity of the Labrador picnic area
stratcomments: Upper Burdigalian
primary_reference: M. Crusafont Pairo and J. Truyols Santonja. 1957. Descubrimiento del primer yacimiento de mamiferos Miocenicos del la Cuenca Valenciana. Notas y Comunicaciones del Instituto Geologico y Minero de Espana 48:3-22

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of St. Vallier. Site is North East of the village, 225 above the Rhone Valley. On the Montrebut plateau.
stratcomments: Late Pliocene, Villafranchian, Villanyian.
primary_reference: M. J. Viret, E. Schmid, and C. Kraehenbuehl. 1954. Le Loess a bancs durcis de Saint-Vallier (Drome) et sa faune de Mammiferes Villafranchiens. Nouvelles Archives du Museum d'Histoire Naturelle de Lyon 4:1-197
collection_comments: The first mention of a site at St Vallier is in 1855 in papers held at Lyon Museum. The data entered here are from the full 1954 site report, most specimens are held in Lyon, a few are kept in Basle.
taxonomy_comments: Most cranial and dental specimens are illustrated in the plates of the 1954 volume. Numbers of specimens have been pieced together from the text and plates and they are likely to under-estimate the true numbers of each species.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Chilhac.
stratcomments: Late Pliocene, Villafranchian, Villanyian.
primary_reference: E. Heintz, C. Guerin, and R. Martin, F. Prat. 1974. Principaux gisements villafranchiens de France: Listes fauniques et biostratigraphie. Memoires du Bureau de Recherches Geologiques et Minieres  78(1):169-182
collection_comments: This site was first discovered in 1875. In 1970 two further sites were discovered which are now designated Chilhac 2 and 3.
taxonomy_comments: Bout (1960) is an unpublished PhD thesis. The faunal list is given in Bonifay, 1991. The original list also gives Hyaena sp., cf. Capreolus sp. and Deperetia ardea DEP. These are not listed by Heintz and have probably been revised. The specimens referred to these species are not discussed in Heintz et al. 1974, whereas all the others can be assigned to updated genera.

geogcomments: There are 2 fossil sites at Ceyssaguet, this is Ceyssaguet 1 on the South flank of a volcano called 'La Banaria'. The co-ordinates are based on the town of Lavoute sur Loire.
stratcomments: Late Early Pleistocene, approximately 1Ma, older than Vallonnet.
primary_reference: E. Bonifay. 1991. Les Premieres industries du Sud-Est de la France et du Massif-Central.  In E. Bonifay, B. Vandermeersch (eds.), Les Premiers Europeens. Edition du comite des Travaux Historiques et Scientifiques (CTHS)
geology_comments: Two episodes of deposition - very degraded aeolian loess, followed by river gravels and grey clays. The first indicates cold temperatures (also indicated by the fauna), whilst the latter is indicative of a more temperate climate.
collection_comments: Site found in 1980 by M.F and E. Bonifay. No faunal list for Ceyssaguet 2 is given - locality is inside the ancient volcanic crater.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Blanzac.
stratcomments: Late Early Pleistocene - approximately 1.0 Ma-0.8 Ma.
primary_reference: E. Bonifay. 1991. Les Premieres industries du Sud-Est de la France et du Massif-Central.  In E. Bonifay, B. Vandermeersch (eds.), Les Premiers Europeens. Edition du comite des Travaux Historiques et Scientifiques (CTHS)
collection_comments: The locality has been known since the 19th century and this is the original faunal list. Excavations have taken place since the 1970s with beds A-F being recognised, collectively known as Soleilhac centre. This faunal list has been entered seperately.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Cesseras.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene (has Ursus deningeri), at the end of the Mindel Complex. \r\n
lithdescript: Four beds J, K, L, M have yielded mammal fossils and archaeology.

lithdescript.1: Four beds J, K, L, M have yielded mammal fossils and archaeology.
collection_comments: Material is held in the Musee d'Anthropologie Prehistorique de Monaco.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Cesseras.

lithdescript.1: Four beds J, K, L, M have yielded mammal fossils and archaeology.
collection_comments: Material is held in the Musee d'Anthropologie Prehistorique de Monaco.

stratcomments: Mittelmain-Komplex, Cromer-time, younger than the fauna of Mauer, older than the main fauna of Mosbach
primary_reference: W. von Koenigswald and W.-D. Heinrich. 1999. Mittelpleistozane Saugetierfaunen aus Mitteleuropa - der Versuch einer biostratigraphischen Zuordnung. Kaupia 9:53-112

collection_name: Kolyma River, between the mouth of Omolon and Anjuj, Jedoma-Suite
geogcomments: Right bank of Kolyma between the river mouth of Omolon and Anjuj
stratcomments: Upper Pleistocene, Riß/Würm and Würm; Jedoma-Suite is elder than Early Alassy deposits and Aljoschka-Suite
lithdescript: big pile of ferruginous fine silty sand and loam layers
