collection_name: La grotte de l'Escale first faunal list, Saint-Esteve-Janson, Bouches du Rhone.
geogcomments: Near Saint-Esteve-Janson.
stratcomments: Early Middle Pleistocene (roughly 0.8-0.7Ma), but originally (in this paper), described as Late Pliocene).
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay and E. Bonifay. 1963. Un gisement a faune epi-villafranchienne a Saint-Esteve-Janson (Bouches-du-Rhone). Comptes Rendue Academie des Sciences, Paris 246:1136-1138
collection_comments: Site found in 1960 by engineers from the French Electricity company. Excavated in 1960, 1961 and 1962. Beds B-G have fossils within them, but all are lumped together in this initial list.
taxonomy_comments: Virtually all faunal assignments in this paper are wrong. See individual beds (A-H) entered as subsets of this one for the full (taxonomically up-to-date) faunal list.

collection_name: La grotte de l'Escale Upper bed B, Saint-Esteve-Janson, Bouches du Rhone.
geogcomments: Near the village of Saint-Esteve-Janson, found during the building of the canal EDF in 1960.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene (Biharian), roughly 0.8-0.7Ma. \r\nThere are a number of beds within the cave - from A (lowermost)-L (topmost), not all of which contain fauna. Each faunal level has been entered separately.
lithdescript: Fauna comes from the upper part of bed B. Bed is described as a whole - it contains deteriorated (altere) browny-reddish fallen rocks, with reddish clayey silts. The whole bed is 1-2 metres thick.
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1973. Principaux gisements paleontologiques Francais du Pleistocene Moyen: Essai de classification. Le Quaternaire
lithdescript.1: Fauna comes from the upper part of bed B. Bed is described as a whole - it contains deteriorated (altere) browny-reddish fallen rocks, with reddish clayey silts. The whole bed is 1-2 metres thick.
collection_comments: Specimens held in the Maison Mediterraneenne des Sciences de l'Homme (MMSH), Aix-en-Provence.\r\nBirds are mentioned but not listed.
taxonomy_comments: Fauna of upper bed B is indicative of a cold-temperate environment.

collection_name: La grotte de l'Escale lower bed B, Saint-Esteve-Janson, Bouches du Rhone.
geogcomments: Near the village of Saint-Esteve-Janson, found during the building of the canal EDF in 1960.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene (Biharian), roughly 0.8-0.7Ma. \r\nThere are a number of beds within the cave - from A (lowermost)-L (topmost), not all of which contain fauna. Each faunal level has been entered separately.
lithdescript: Fauna comes from the lower part of bed B. Bed is described as a whole - it contains deteriorated (altere) browny-reddish fallen rocks, with reddish clayey silts. The whole bed is 1-2 metres thick.
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1973. Principaux gisements paleontologiques Francais du Pleistocene Moyen: Essai de classification. Le Quaternaire
lithdescript.1: Fauna comes from the lower part of bed B. Bed is described as a whole - it contains deteriorated (altere) browny-reddish fallen rocks, with reddish clayey silts. The whole bed is 1-2 metres thick.

taxonomy_comments: Fauna of lower bed B is indicative of a temperate and humid/wet environment.

collection_name: Abbeville (Somme River terrace), Pas de Calais
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the town of Abbeville.
stratcomments: An interglacial at the start of the Mindel (Mindel 1 or 2). Middle Pleistocene, roughly 0.8-0.6Ma.
lithdescript: The fauna from the grey marl is difficult to separate from the nearby gravels (which lie above and beneath it).
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1973. Principaux gisements paleontologiques Francais du Pleistocene Moyen: Essai de classification. Le Quaternaire
lithdescript.1: The fauna from the grey marl is difficult to separate from the nearby gravels (which lie above and beneath it).
geology_comments: The high terrace of the River Somme.
collection_comments: Type site for the Acheulian (Abbevillian)

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Montmaurin.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene, doubtfully assigned to the Mindel-Riss (roughly 0.6-0.45Ma), but needs more work to ascertain this.
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1973. Principaux gisements paleontologiques Francais du Pleistocene Moyen: Essai de classification. Le Quaternaire
collection_comments: Found and excavated in 1902.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Montmaurin.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the island itself, in the sea just off Marseille.
stratcomments: Generally Middle Pleistocene, although possibly Wurm (Late Pleistocene).
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1973. Principaux gisements paleontologiques Francais du Pleistocene Moyen: Essai de classification. Le Quaternaire
collection_comments: Described briefly by Marion in 1883.
taxonomy_comments: A small bear, possibly Plionarctos.

geogcomments: Co-ordinate based on the nearby village of Le Beausset (site location estimated from map in Bonifay, 1973).
stratcomments: Late Lower to Early Middle Pleistocene.

collection_name: Grotte de l'Eglise, Cenac-et-Saint-Julien, Dordogne, Aquitaine

stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene, Middle Mindel (roughly 0.65-0.58Ma). There are 17 beds within the site, but this one of the first faunal lists and they are not subdivided.

collection_comments: A rescue dig conducted by F. Prat in 1966. Probably the same site as that reported by Harle and Stehlin in 1913. Most of the 17 beds contain fossils.

collection_name: Mestas de Con, Cangas de Onis, Asturias
geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Mestas de Con.
stratcomments: Latest Lower Pleistocene/Early Middle Pleistocene. Early Elsterian = Mindel glaciation.
primary_reference: M. Crusafont Pairo. 1960. Le Quaternaire Espagnol et sa faune de mammiferes - essai de synthese. Anthropos, supplement 1 (Mammalia Pleistocaenica) 1960(1):55-64
taxonomy_comments: Homotherium crenatidens originally described as Machairodus by Fraga 1955.

collection_name: Olivola Fauna in Lunigiana district, NW Tuscany
geogcomments: Locality is on a road leading to Olivola village (on which the co-ordinates are based), which is on a hilltop to the North East of Aulla, the main town of Lunigiana district.
stratcomments: Latest Pliocene/Earliest Pleistocene. Olivola fauna unit (roughly 1.8Ma).
lithdescript: pink-yellowish matrix.
primary_reference: A. Azzaroli. 2001. Middle and Late Villafranchian vertebrates from Tuscany and Umbria. A synopsis. Bollettino della Societa Paleontologica Italiana 40(3):351-356
lithdescript.1: pink-yellowish matrix.
geology_comments: Fossils washed in by a flood.
collection_comments: First collected by Igino Cocchi and Giovanni Cappellini (material in Pisa and Bologna Universities). Main collection by Forsyth Major in late 1800s (material in Florence University, Palaeontology Museum and The Natural History Museum, London) (possibly also some in Paris).

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the town of Collecurti, in the Apennines. Not far from the watershed between the Umbria and Marche districts.
stratcomments: Jaramillo event (roughly 1.0-0.95Ma). Early-Middle Pleistocene. Villafranchian-Galerian turnover, Early Biharian.
primary_reference: J. Madurell-Malapeira, S. Bartolini-Lucenti, and M. Prat-Vericat, L. Sorbelli, A. Blasetti, M. P. Ferretti, A. Goro, M. Cherin. 2022. Jaramillo-aged carnivorans from Collecurti (Colfiorito Basin, Italy). Historical Biology 34(10):1928-1940
geology_comments: Lacustrine sedimentary basin.
collection_comments: 'The micromammal remains of Colfiorito are represented by a number of very shattered, unrecognizable, and thus meaningless, bony fragments and by a few teeth, some of which, instead, are decisive from both systematical and stratigraphical points of view.'
taxonomy_comments: Only the micromammal remains are described in Ficcarelli et al. 1990.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of Meleto.

lithdescript: clay.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on Pakefield, Kessingland is slightly to the south. The locality is in a sea cliff exposed between the two villages.
stratcomments: Early Middle Pleistocene. Ferruginous gravels layer, overlain by the rootlet bed.
lithdescript: Red-Brown sands and gravels ('ferruginous gravels'), 2+ metres thick.
primary_reference: A. J. Stuart and A. M. Lister. 2001. The mammalian faunas of Pakefield/Kessingland and Corton, Suffolk, UK: evidence for a new temperate episode in the British early Middle Pleistocene. Quaternary Science Reviews 20:1677-1692
lithdescript.1: Red-Brown sands and gravels ('ferruginous gravels'), 2+ metres thick.
geology_comments: well stratified sands and gravels. 'The restricted lateral extent of this deposit strongly suggests one or more river channel fills.'
collection_comments: First collected and published by Blake in 1884, 1890.
taxonomy_comments: Temperate fauna.

geogcomments: Co-ordinates based on the village of West Runton.
stratcomments: Early Middle Pleistocene. Cromerian stratotype. Pollen substage 2. PJW: Was Freshwater Bed.
primary_reference: A. J. Stuart. 1995. Insularity and Quaternary vertebrate faunas in Britain and Ireland. In R. C. Preece, ed., Island Britain: a Quaternary perspective. Geological Society Special Publication No. 96, London.
collection_comments: Temperate climate. Temperate woodland, grass and herbs with alder carr, reedswamp and aquatic vegetation close by.

geogcomments: Central Rhineland, close to the confluence of the Rhine and Moselle. The site is on a small spur of land on the eastern bank of the Nette Stream.
stratcomments: Oldest Arvicola cantianus fauna, Middle Middle Pleistocene, Latest Cromerian - correlated with interglacial IV, roughly 0.4Ma. Material has been collected from a variety of units, but for the mammals they are all regarded as the same faunal unit. Several specimens in units G and F refit together. Mollusca have been subdivided but are not given here.
primary_reference: T. van Kolfschoten and E. Turner. 1996. Early Middle Pleistocene mammalian faunas from Karlich (Kaerlich) and Miesenheim I and their biostratigraphical implications. In C. Turner , ed., The early Middle Pleistocene in Europe, Balkema, Rotterdam
collection_comments: Mollusca collected but not listed here. Site discovered after pumice extraction in 1982 and excavated between 1982 and 1990.
taxonomy_comments: Mammalian faunal list for all levels.

stratcomments: Late Early Pleistocene, post Jaramillo Event, pre Brunhes/Matuyama boundary.

stratcomments: Early Pleistocene.

collection_comments: Site found in April 1953, and first published in 1953. 25 isolated teeth were found in the first instance and compared in date to Val d'Arno, Tegelen, St Vallier, etc.

geogcomments: In Venosa basin, co-ordinates based on the village of Venosa.
stratcomments: Somewhat heterogenous fauna - some Villafranchian, but others Middle Pleistocene. Mindelian according to the abstract. Later than Vallonnet (Late Early Pleistocene).\r\n
primary_reference: M. F. Bonifay. 1977. Liste preliminaire de la grande faune du gisement prehistorique de Venosa. Bulletin du Musee d'Anthropologie prehistorique de Monaco 21:115-125
collection_comments: Specimens excavated by the Musee d'Anthropologie prehistorique de Monaco in collaboration with the Institut italien de Paleontologie humaine a Venosa-Loreto.
taxonomy_comments: This publication only lists large mammals. It doesn't state whether any small mammal remains have been recovered.

collection_name: Vallonnet Cave bed 3 'faunal level', Roquebrune-Cap-Martin, Alpes Maritimes
geogcomments: Site is a cave near Roquebrune-cap-Martin on which the co-ordinates have been based.
stratcomments: Dated by ESR between 980,000 to 910,000 years ago (Jaramillo event). Late Early Pleistocene. Mammalian fauna is found in level 3, known as the 'Faunal horizon of Vallonnet'. There are three levels within member 3 - B1, B2 and C, but the large mammal faunal list is not subdivided.
lithdescript: sandy clayey-muds, rich in stoneas and pebbles. Red in colour and 30 to 100cm thick.
primary_reference: H. de Lumley, H-D. Kahlke, and A.-M. Moigne, P.-E. Moulle. 1988. Les Faunes de grands mammiferes de la Grotte du Vallonnet, Roquebrune-Cap-Martin, Alpes-Maritimes. L'Anthropologie 92(2):465-496
lithdescript.1: sandy clayey-muds, rich in stoneas and pebbles. Red in colour and 30 to 100cm thick.
geology_comments: 5 stratigraphic units at the site. Bed 3 is further subdivided into levels B1, B2 and C, but the large mammal fauna is given as a list for the entire bed. The first level (C) correponds to a very dry and cold climate, whilst the upper (B1) is progressively more temperate and humid.

geogcomments: Panxian Dadong is located in Panxian District, Liupanshui City, Guizhou Province. On the western side of the Guizhou Plateau.
stratcomments: Middle Pleistocene. U-series dating of travertine gives a minimum age for the deposit of 300,000BP.
primary_reference: W. Huang, X. Si, and Y. Hou, S. Miller-Antonio, L. A. Schepartz. 1995. Excavations at Panxian Dadong, Guizhou Province, Southern China. Current Anthropology 36(5):844-846
geology_comments: Dadong is the Middle level cave in a series of three interconnecting caverns stacked within a 230m high hill. The hill itself is situated in a small valley whose floor has an elevation of 1,630m above sea level. The cave entrance is 55m wide and 50m high, faces east, and lies at an elevation of 32.5m above the valley floor. It is a large cavern (Dadong = Grand cave), the main hall measures 220m from front to back and covers 8000 square metres.
collection_comments: 92 square metres of deposit were excavated.

geogcomments: 25 km west of Istanbul; (Istanbul, Turkey; approximate geographical coordinates 40.989324, 28.775374) (Otriazhyi et al 2025)
stratcomments: Khersonian
lithdescript: Above, a layer of fine, micaceous sands, up to 6 to 8 m thick; 3" this layer of sand is crossed, towards the bottom, by a bed, sometimes doubled, a few decimeters thick, composed of gravel and puddingstone or breccia whose elements (flint or phosphate nodules) go up to the size of a fist at most; all cemented by an iron oxide with traces of manganese. It is this bed which is the most productive, as well as, for large pieces, the surrounding sand, mainly towards the contact with the clay;

geogcomments: "POSITION: the village Patara Dmanisi (Dmanisi's region), in the confluence of rivers Mashavera and Pinezauri, 65 km south-westwards from Tbilisi, Eastern Georgia." (pg. 152). Coordinates are for town of Dmanisi.\r\n"excavations total approximately 150 m2" but the hominids are from Site 1, which is 16 m2 (Gabunia et al. 2000)
stratcomments: underlying Masavera Basalt was dated at 1.8 +/- 0.1 Ma (K-Ar) by Gabunia and Vekua 1995 and at 1.85 +/- 0.01 (40Ar/39Ar) by Gabunia et al. 2000 and is of normal polarity, so it correlates with the Olduvai Subchron; unit A also is normally polarized and assigned to the same subchron, whereas unit B and fossiliferous fissure fills in unit A are reversed and assigned to the following Matuyama Subchron (Gabunia et al. 2000)
lithdescript: "2.5 m of fossiliferous, volcaniclastic alluvium" consisting of units A (lower) and B (upper), with most fossils coming from fills in unit A that are "poorly lithified and distinctively different in color, texture, and primary structures from the surrounding unit A sediment" that consists of tuffaceous loamy sand (Gabunia et al. 2000)
primary_reference: L. Gabunia, A. Vekua, and D. Lordkipanidze, C. C. Swisher III, R. Ferring, A. Justus, M. Nioradze, M. Tvalchrelidze, S. C. Antón, G. Bosinski, O. Jöris, M.-A. de Lumley, Givi Majsuradze, Aleksander Mouskhelishvili. 2000. Earliest Pleistocene hominid cranial remains from Dmanisi, Republic of Georgia: taxonomy, geological setting, and age. Science 288:1019-1025
lithdescript.1: "2.5 m of fossiliferous, volcaniclastic alluvium" consisting of units A (lower) and B (upper), with most fossils coming from fills in unit A that are "poorly lithified and distinctively different in color, texture, and primary structures from the surrounding unit A sediment" that consists of tuffaceous loamy sand (Gabunia et al. 2000)
geology_comments: "lens- to tunnel-shaped... intrusive fills within unit A strata that formed at the time of erosion of unit A deposits but before deposition of unit B... These structures probably formed by underground water movement or soil piping, which eroded subsurface tunnels and cavities" (Gabunia et al. 2000)
collection_comments: "over 2000 identifiable vertebrate fossils... and over 1000 stone artifacts" (Gabunia et al. 2000)

lithdescript: "sand and clay"

lithdescript.1: "sand and clay"

taxonomy_comments: revises original descriptions of Cope (1878, 1884, 1889) and Matthew (1902)

geogcomments: bones were found in "old solution passages of variable diameter not exceeding a few feet" in a "limestone quarry" that is "about three-quarters of a mile south of the present entrance to Craighead Caverns on the property of Mr. George Kyle" (coordinate based on Craighead Caverns)
stratcomments: "a typical Pleistocene fauna of this region... certainly post-Pliocene and pre-historic"
lithdescript: "red clay, typical cave-earth of the region, with a few broken limestone fragments and some secondary deposition of calcite"
primary_reference: G. G. Simpson. 1941. Discovery of jaguar bones and footprints in a cave in Tennessee. American Museum Novitates 1131:1-12
lithdescript.1: "red clay, typical cave-earth of the region, with a few broken limestone fragments and some secondary deposition of calcite"
geology_comments: a "Pleistocene fissure formation"
collection_comments: some bones were "picked up by workmen in the quarry" and others were found "in place" during a visit by Simpson

geogcomments: "at the base of a limestone cliff on the east side of the Schell Creek Range... The cave extends over 137 m into the mountain along a fault plane and consists of one main passage" and BC-1 and BC-2 are "approximately 10 m apart at the rear of the cave" within pools (coordinate based on averaged values for North Schell Peak and South Schell Peak)
stratcomments: two mid-Holocene radiocarbon dates on bear bones are rejected by Emslie and Czaplewski (1985) and the cave is believed to have been "sealed since at least the close of the Pleistocene"; Emslie and Mead (2020) report uncorrected 14C date for Ursus from BC-1 of 22,321+/-110 and calibrated range in calendar years BP of 27,011–26,217
lithdescript: bones were "partially buried in silt"

geogcomments: "on the east slope of the Guadalupe Mountains... The entrance, which faces east of south, is about seventy feet above the floor of the [unspecified] canyon... the cave [is] roughly fifty feet from the entrance to the rear wall, and thirteen feet in width at a point where the talus slope began inside the entrance" (Schultz and Howard 1935); "Sec. 35, R. 21 E, T. 22 S, Eddy Co. [i.e., very close to Three Forks]" (Jones 1959)
stratcomments: "the depth of dirt and debris" ranges from between "two or three feet" and "approximately nine feet" deep; "the surface of the cave showed disturbance" by "local collectors" who "had discovered several burials... in the upper two or three feet... Below the burial level many hearths or lens-shaped deposits of charcoal and ashes were found at various levels down to a depth of eight feet six inches. Also in these lower levels were found the bones... in a number of cases, associated directly with the hearths"
lithdescript: a "long continued accumulation of wind-blown dust together with rock fragments, which had fallen from the roof and walls"
primary_reference: C. B. Schultz and E. B. Howard. 1935. The fauna of Burnet Cave, Guadalupe Mountains, New Mexico. Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia 87:273-298
lithdescript.1: a "long continued accumulation of wind-blown dust together with rock fragments, which had fallen from the roof and walls"
collectors: C. B. Schultz, E. B. Howard, B. Brown
collection_comments: Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia collection (through 1935) and UNSM collection (1937, 1938, and possibly other years)\r\nBrown was a visitor for one week in 1931 only

geogcomments: within the town of Hot Springs (basis of coordinate)
stratcomments: "a lense-shaped deposit" ranging from 14 to 21 m in thickness, with fossils occuring in "all the sedimentary phases"\na radiocarbon date on in situ "bone apatite" of "26,075 +975 -790 B.P." is considered "minimal" although it is from "approximately one half of the fill depth"
lithdescript: "The sinkhole fill consists of laminated, fine-grained materials, ranging from clay to coarse sand" derived from "reworking and sorting of decomposed wall rock and terrace gravels, with some cobbles and boulders introduced by wall collapse"
primary_reference: L. D. Agenbroad. 1985. The Hot Springs Mammoth Site, South Dakota: from salvage paleontology to in situ exhibit and research center. Dakoterra 2(2):121-139

geology_comments: "a natural trap in the form of a karst depression [i.e., sinkhole] containing a spring-fed pond"
collection_comments: "discovered accidentally during... land leveling in the summer of 1974 in preparation for a housing development" and there was extensive quarrying in later seasons as well as recovery of "microvertebrate remains" by "screen wash"

geogcomments: "40º 58' 30" N, 83º 27' 00" W... part of a cave system that includes Hendricks Cave and Indian Trail Caverns"
stratcomments: faunal remains and artifacts are from "two layers of cave breccia (10 and 45 thick, respectively"\nfive dates range from "11,710 +/- 220" on "bulk wood charcoal" to "11,060 +/- 60" on a bone "side scraper"
lithdescript: "greasy, dark-gray, organic-rich... silt and sand" forms "the matrix of the breccia," which includes "boulders and cobbles" that are composed of "porous, stromatolitic, Silurian dolomite"; the two breccia layers are separated by a "wedge-shaped, banded, alluvial channel" that includes "alternating layers of gray silty-clay and red sand"
primary_reference: K. B. Tankersley. 1997. Sheriden: a Clovis cave site in eastern North America. 12(6):713-724

geology_comments: cave deposits that "display evidence of alluvial transport"
collection_comments: Cincinnati Museum of Natural History collection (apparently)\r\n"All of the sediment was water-screened through a fine mesh"
taxonomy_comments: "more than 60 species of large and small vertebrates" are present but only key mammals are mentioned

geogcomments: "along the Porcupine River in northeastern Alaska... in the Lower Rampart region" apparently close to "Rampart canyon... 25 m above a scoured late-Pleistocene coulee, and ca. 45 m above the base level of the Porcupine River" (coordinate based on Rampart Gorge)
stratcomments: "sampling units were removed in 5- or 10-cm thick levels" in 1978 and "at 5-cm-thick intervals" in 1987\nunit A is not dated but immediately overlies A/B contact dates of 3470 +/- 90 and 3510 +/- 100 on charcoal
lithdescript: "Holocene 'upper units' include Units A, B, and Ba and are brown organic breccia with unweathered clasts. The Pleistocene 'lower units' include Units C and D and are light-brown to gray calcareous breccia with weathered clasts and include faunal remains of extinct megafauna"

collection_comments: in 1987 "sediments were sieved in the field through nested shaker screens with 1/4 and 1/8 in. mesh"

collection_name: Mississippi Lime and Material Company Plant No. 2
geogcomments: "bluffs northwest of Alton" at "a height of from 125 to 175 feet above the Mississippi River" (coordinate based on Alton)\r\nmost specimens were found in a single quarry
stratcomments: age of loess is thought to be either "Sangamon" or (younger and therefore) "Peorian"
lithdescript: "In the contact zone between the [glacial, 1-3 feet thick] till and the [reddish] loess" in the local section; the loess has "no notable quantity of sand or suggestion of stratification" while the till is "reddish [and] contains many erratic pebbles of granite, dolerite, greenstone, quartzite, and other Canadian rocks, and also some local rocks"; the glacial till lies immediately over a limestone
primary_reference: M. M. Leighton. 1921. The Pleistocene succession near Alton, Illinois, and the age of the mammalian fossil fauna. Journal of Geology 29(6):505-514
lithdescript.1: "In the contact zone between the [glacial, 1-3 feet thick] till and the [reddish] loess" in the local section; the loess has "no notable quantity of sand or suggestion of stratification" while the till is "reddish [and] contains many erratic pebbles of granite, dolerite, greenstone, quartzite, and other Canadian rocks, and also some local rocks"; the glacial till lies immediately over a limestone
geology_comments: the "loess" is "doubtless aeolian in origin" but the fossils are thought to have been entrapped in "ponds" formed in "a gentle depression at the top of the limestone," which makes sense only if the sequence was glaciation followed by pond formation/fossilization followed by loess deposition
collection_comments: McAdams collection was made "Some time previous to 1883"
taxonomy_comments: mammal identifications were made by O. P. Hay (may the buyer beware)\r\nPolygyra prounda pleistocenica Baker (new ssp.) Polygyra multilineata altonensis Baker (n. ssp.) are both found throughout the lower loess overlying the fossiliferous zone

geogcomments: from "a gigantic borrow pit near the west anchor of the [Lewisville] damsite... in extreme southeast Denton County, on the west side of the Elm Fork of Trinity River, just north of Lewisville" (coordinate based on Lewisville Dam)
stratcomments: the pit "removed some 20 feet of earth from the local [Upper Shuler subformation of the] Pemberton Hill-Lewisville (T-2) terrace surface" (thought to be Peorian = mid-Wisconsinan in age) and hearths were recovered from layers 1, 2, 3, and 5 in the local sequence of seven layers, each capped by "a minor uncomformity and caliche formation"; there are radiocarbon dates on "charred, fibrous, vegetable material" and "remains of 4 small logs" of "more than 37,000 years B.P." from two distinct hearth sites in layer 2
lithdescript: "yellow sandy clay"

stratcomments: No direct dating is available for the fossil localities, but the occurrence of marine vertebrates and the inclusion of marine-originated sediments indicate that the fossil-bearing layers had been deposited near the past sea level (Nakajima and Kuwano 1957; Nakajima 1958; Hasegawa et al. 1988). Therefore, the localities can be correlated to certain marine terrace surfaces; Loc. 2 (70–80 m above sea level) can be correlated to the Toei surface (65–80 m; MIS 9).

geogcomments: "Maba town, Qujiang district, Shaoguan city... Lion Rock is composed of two isolated mountains connected through subsurface streams: Lion Head and Lion Tail Mountains. The Maba human remains were found in the cave of Lion Head Mountain"
stratcomments: "The cave contained three strata... filling a complex of fissures and openings. Maba 1 and a diversity of mammalian fossils were found in the second level. These deposits extended as much as 10 m above the first level and were ∼8 m in length, ∼7 m in width, and ∼6 m in total height... The associated faunal remains... indicate a later Middle or Late Pleistocene age for the Maba cranium (9– 11). A uranium series date on associated vertebrate teeth yielded an age of 129,000–135,000 y before present (yBP) (12), but it is not clear whether this determination accurately dates the cranium" because of possible analytical biases; "More recent 230Th/234U dating of capping flowstone samples from Southern Branch Cave, another chamber in Lion Head Mountain, suggests that some of the Maba deposits may be as old as 237,000 yBP"
lithdescript: "primarily... yellow brown clays"
primary_reference: X.-J. Wu, L. A. Schepartz, and W. Liu, E. Trinkaus. 2011. Antemortem trauma and survival in the late Middle Pleistocene human cranium from Maba, South China. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
lithdescript.1: "primarily... yellow brown clays"
geology_comments: "a karst cave"

stratcomments: The two OSL ages from unit A1 (118 13, 105 10 ka; Table 2) are on the young side of the Eemian (c. 130–115 ka), but underestimation of quartz OSL ages from Eemian deposits has been described from other sites (e.g. Murray & Funder 2003; Murray et al. 2007; Lowick et al. 2010). The age underestima- tion may be of the order of 10%, or more, and is believed to be at least partly caused by sensitivity changes during measurement. An interglacial origin for the deposit is also supported by its relatively warm foraminiferal fauna (Bergsten et al. 1998). Our age estimation for unit A1 suggests an Eemian age for the subfossil polar bear mandible retrieved from the lower part of unit A (Ingólfsson & Wiig 2009), now unit A1.\r\n

collection_aka: Rudabánya 1 ("Mastodon Wall"), Rudabánya 2 ("Gibraltar"), Rudabánya 3 ("The Great Wall"), Rudabánya 4, Rudabánya 5, Rudabánya 6, Rudabánya 7
geogcomments: The Rudabánya locality is situated at the transition from the Inner Western Carpathians (represented by the Rudabánya Hills) to the Pannonian Basin. The fossils were collected in iron ore open pits and mines (the younger and larger ones named Andrassy I, Andrassy II, Andrassy III and Vilmos; see map in fig. 2 of Nemeth et al., 2013, Carp. J. Earth Envir. Sci. 8(1):47-58) from the soft Cenozoic cover of the diagenetically limonitized and sideritized target rocks which are mainly Triassic in age.\r\ncoordinate is for abandoned Andrassy II and Vilmos mines about 3 km NE of village of Rudabánya
stratcomments: "A synthesis of the biochronologic bases for Rudabánya II’s age strongly supports a late MN 9, Pannonian F correlation, ca. 10-9.7 Ma" (Bernor et al., 2004). All other sublocalities are regarded to be contemporaneous (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nAccording to Nemeth et al. (2013) the Pannonian sediments of the Rudabánya mining area are referred to as Edelény Formation.
lithdescript: "Rudabánya’s sediments include cyclic layers of gray marl, clay and lignite, totalling 8-12 meters in thickness. These sediments, include rich fossiliferous levels that contain plant impressions, seeds, molluscs, vertebrate remains and, of especial note, catarrhine primates" (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nFor a more elaborate description of the sediments of the Rudabanya 2 sublocality see Andrews & Cameron (2010).
primary_reference: R. L. Bernor, L. Kordos L. Rook et al. 2004. Recent Advances on Multidisciplinary Research at Rudabánya, Late Miocene (MN9), Hungary: a compendium. Palaeontographia Italica 89:3-36
lithdescript.1: "Rudabánya’s sediments include cyclic layers of gray marl, clay and lignite, totalling 8-12 meters in thickness. These sediments, include rich fossiliferous levels that contain plant impressions, seeds, molluscs, vertebrate remains and, of especial note, catarrhine primates" (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nFor a more elaborate description of the sediments of the Rudabanya 2 sublocality see Andrews & Cameron (2010).
geology_comments: Deposition took place in different "lacustrine and peri-lacustrine" (swamp forests etc.) environments (Bernor et al., 2004) on a low-angle slope at the periphery of the Pannonian Lake. A detailed reconstruction of the paleoenvironments of Rudabánya is given in Andrews & Cameron (2010, Palaeogeogr., Palaeoclimatol., Palaeoecol. 297(2):311-329).
collection_methods: surface (in situ),sieve,field collection,survey of museum collection
collectors: G. Pethö, G. Hernyák, M. Kretzoi, L. Kordos, D.R. Begun, R. L. Bernor, M. Armour-Chelu, P. Andrews,
collection_comments: The first known vertebrate was found in 1902 by G. Pethö. A few important discoveries were made by G. Hernyák, the chief geologist of the Vilmos mine, in the 1960s and later on. First systematic collecting took place in the 1970s under the aegis of M. Kretzoi. After 1990 several paleontologists from the western hemisphere joined the excavations. For a more detailed review of the research history at Rudabánya see Bernor et al. (2004).\r\nAll or at least the bulk of the Rudabánya material is housed in the Geological and Geophysical Institute of Hungary (hung.: Magyar Földtani és Geofizikai Intézet, formerly Magyar Állami Földtani Intézet, abbreviated MAFI) in Budapest.
taxonomy_comments: The emphasis of the paper by Bernor et al. (2004) is on the Rudabánya 2 (also spelled "Rudabánya II") sublocality but most co-authors list taxa from all sublocalities without giving particularities on specimens and/or sites. List contains taxa that may have been cited by earlier authors under a different name and/or combination and some names cited in earlier works may have fallen into synonymy with those cited in the list (may already be noted in the list). Determinations in Bernor et al. (2004): E. Krolopp (molluscs); Z. Roček (amphibians); Z. Szyndlar (snakes); D. Janossy (birds); G. Topal (bats); L. Kordos and D. R. Begun (primates); G. Daxner-Höck, O. Fejfar, and L. Kordos (rodents); L. Werdelin (carnivorans); M. Gasparik (proboscideans); R. L. Bernor, M. Armour-Chely, T. Kaiser, R. Scott, L. de Bonis, J. Franzen, and K. Heissig (perissodactyls); M. Fortelius, R. L. Bernor, N. Fessaha, M. Armour-Chelu, and A. Gentry (artiodactyls). The list only contains vertebrate taxa mentioned in the text of Bernor et al. (2004) and ignores the faunal list given in table 3 (pp. 22-24) since it is partially contradictory to the informations given in the text (e.g. the table lists "?Leptotyphlopidae gen. et sp. indet." whereas Szyndlar in his section on snakes explicitly states that the only vertebra clearly referrable to Scolecophidia may belong to Typhlops and that the presence of leptotyphlopids in Rudabánya is very unlikely). In the taxon list in table 3 there appear 5 chelonian taxa which are not mentioned elsewhere in the paper: Trionyx sp., Geomyda sp., Testudo cf. kalksburgensis, Testudo sp. I, and Testudo sp. II.

stratcomments: "Since their publication in the ‘‘International Symposium on Mammalian Biostratigraphy and Palaeoecology’’ (Engesser and Mayo 1987), Rickenbach is definitively recognised as the European mammalian reference locality for level MP29 (...) Detailed correlations between Swiss mammal levels and magnetostratigraphy were additionally published by Schlunegger et al. (1996), who clearly correlated the Rickenbach level (=MP29) within the chron 6 Cr, which actually corresponds to the interval 23.4–23.9 Ma (Fig. 1; Berger 2011)" (from Mennecart et al. 2012)\r\n\r\nde Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The age of the Rickenbach deposits, 23.9–23.4 Ma (berger 2011), corresponds to a major faunal remodeling known as the “Microbunodon Event” (scHerler et al. 2013, Mennecart 2015).
lithdescript: de Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The Rickenbach quarry (Canton of Solothurn, Swit- zerland, Fig. 1) was first mined during the 19th century for its fine sands of the Eocene filling a karstic fissure in Jurassic limestone. Soon after, Martin (1906) described the first remains of a latest Oligocene fauna. These fossils were embedded in 4 to 8 m molasse sandstone (Knauermolasse) overlaying the Eocene sediments. Mammal remains are especially concentrated in the basal conglomerate (Mennecart, pers. obs.).
primary_reference: B. Mennecart, L. Scherler, and F. Hiard, D. Becker, JP Berger. 2012. Large mammals from Rickenbach (Switzerland, reference locality MP29, Late Oligocene): biostratigraphic and palaeoenvironmental implications. Swiss Journal of Palaeontology 131:161-181
lithdescript.1: de Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The Rickenbach quarry (Canton of Solothurn, Swit- zerland, Fig. 1) was first mined during the 19th century for its fine sands of the Eocene filling a karstic fissure in Jurassic limestone. Soon after, Martin (1906) described the first remains of a latest Oligocene fauna. These fossils were embedded in 4 to 8 m molasse sandstone (Knauermolasse) overlaying the Eocene sediments. Mammal remains are especially concentrated in the basal conglomerate (Mennecart, pers. obs.).
geology_comments: de Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The composition of the Rickenbach fauna is characteristic for arid and wooded savannah environments with a strong seasonality

stratcomments: "Since their publication in the ‘‘International Symposium on Mammalian Biostratigraphy and Palaeoecology’’ (Engesser and Mayo 1987), Rickenbach is definitively recognised as the European mammalian reference locality for level MP29 (...) Detailed correlations between Swiss mammal levels and magnetostratigraphy were additionally published by Schlunegger et al. (1996), who clearly correlated the Rickenbach level (=MP29) within the chron 6 Cr, which actually corresponds to the interval 23.4–23.9 Ma (Fig. 1; Berger 2011)" (from Mennecart et al. 2012)\r\n\r\nde Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The age of the Rickenbach deposits, 23.9–23.4 Ma (berger 2011), corresponds to a major faunal remodeling known as the “Microbunodon Event” (scHerler et al. 2013, Mennecart 2015).
lithdescript: de Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The Rickenbach quarry (Canton of Solothurn, Swit- zerland, Fig. 1) was first mined during the 19th century for its fine sands of the Eocene filling a karstic fissure in Jurassic limestone. Soon after, Martin (1906) described the first remains of a latest Oligocene fauna. These fossils were embedded in 4 to 8 m molasse sandstone (Knauermolasse) overlaying the Eocene sediments. Mammal remains are especially concentrated in the basal conglomerate (Mennecart, pers. obs.).

lithdescript:
The fossiliferous deposits comprise phosphatic infill of paleokarst of upper Middle and lower Upper Jurassic limestone of the Rocamadour and Staint Gery Formations ("Callovo-Oxfordium"). These phosphatic deposits have been mined in the late 19th and early 20th century. Today the infill of four caves ("galleries") is still preserved in situ. These caves empty at different levels into a 20 m deep shaft-like sinkhole ("puits") and are designated with the letters A to D. Valbro A is at the shallowest level, at about 4 m depth (Peigné et al. 2014).
\r\nThe infill of all four galleries consists of the characteristic red loam known from other Quercy phosphorite localities. It is poor in iron pisolites but relatively rich in fragments of phosphatic rock and limestone (Peigné et al. 2014).
primary_reference: S. Peigné, M. Vianey-Liaud, and T. Pélissié, B. Sigé. 2014. Valbro : un nouveau site à vertébrés de l’Oligocène inférieur (MP22) de France (Quercy). I – Contexte géologique ; Mammalia : Rodentia, Hyaenodontida, Carnivora [Valbro: A new site of vertebrates from the early Oligocene (MP22) of France (Quercy). I – Geological context; Mammalia: Rodentia, Hyaenodontida, Carnivora]. Annales de Paléontologie 100(1):1-45

These caves empty at different levels into a 20 m deep shaft-like sinkhole ("puits") and are designated with the letters A to D. Valbro A is at the shallowest level, at about 4 m depth (Peigné et al. 2014).

collection_comments:
"Fossils were collected directly from the sediment and after screen washing" (Rage & Augé, 2015).
"The material is curated in the Institut des Sciences de l’Évolution (ISEM), Université de Montpellier 2, France" (Rage & Augé, 2015).
First reconnaissance at Valbro took place in 2000, main excavations were conducted in the years 2008, 2009 and 2012 (Peigné et al. 2014).

geogcomments: The Kalamakia site is located on the western coast of the Mani peninsula. Like other sites in this region (Apidima, Lakonis; see Harvati et al., 2003; Panagopoulou et al., 2002e2004; Harvati et al., 2009, 2011), it is a karstic cave formed in the limestone cliffside, and one of the numerous caves opened in this part of the peninsula preserving Pleistocene fillings with dense Paleolithic material (Darlas and Psathi, 2008). The cave is situated directly on the coast approximately 2.5 km northwest of Areopolis (Fig. 1), some 10 m from the current sea-line at 2.5 m above sea level (hereafter asl), and directly above what is interpreted as the Tyrrhenean terrace
primary_reference: K. Harvati, A. Darlas, and S. E. Bailey, T. R. Rein, S. El Zaatari, L. Fiorenza, O. Kullmer, E. Psathi. 2013. New Neanderthal remains from Mani peninsula, Southern Greece: The Kalamakia Middle Paleolithic cave site. Journal of Human Evolution 64(6):486-499

lithdescript: The first unit unconformably overlies the Cretaceous limestones. This unit, about 30 m thick, and mostly formed by lacustrine facies (white-yellowish marls and laminated limestones, and gray lutites) is organized as a deepening sequence. The lacustrine facies laterally inter-  nger with a belt of reddish conglomerates, sandstones, and lutites related to alluvial fans feeding into the basin from the northern and western margins. The entire unit shows a  ning-up evolution. The upper unit, about 70 m thick, con- sists in alluvial facies (brown-reddish conglomerates, sand- stones, and lutites), laterally related to yellow sandstones and lutites with coal and nodules of gypsum.

geogcomments: The Rudabánya locality is situated at the transition from the Inner Western Carpathians (represented by the Rudabánya Hills) to the Pannonian Basin. The fossils were collected in iron ore open pits and mines (the younger and larger ones named Andrassy I, Andrassy II, Andrassy III and Vilmos; see map in fig. 2 of Nemeth et al., 2013, Carp. J. Earth Envir. Sci. 8(1):47-58) from the soft Cenozoic cover of the diagenetically limonitized and sideritized target rocks which are mainly Triassic in age.\r\ncoordinate is for abandoned Andrassy II and Vilmos mines about 3 km NE of village of Rudabánya
stratcomments: "A synthesis of the biochronologic bases for Rudabánya II’s age strongly supports a late MN 9, Pannonian F correlation, ca. 10-9.7 Ma" (Bernor et al., 2004). All other sublocalities are regarded to be contemporaneous (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nAccording to Nemeth et al. (2013) the Pannonian sediments of the Rudabánya mining area are referred to as Edelény Formation.
lithdescript: "Rudabánya’s sediments include cyclic layers of gray marl, clay and lignite, totalling 8-12 meters in thickness. These sediments, include rich fossiliferous levels that contain plant impressions, seeds, molluscs, vertebrate remains and, of especial note, catarrhine primates" (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nFor a more elaborate description of the sediments of the Rudabanya 2 sublocality see Andrews & Cameron (2010).
primary_reference: L. de Bones, J. Abella, and G. Merceron, D. R. Begun. 2017. A new late Miocene ailuropodine (Giant Panda) from Rudaba ́nya (North-central Hungary). Geobios 50:413-421
lithdescript.1: "Rudabánya’s sediments include cyclic layers of gray marl, clay and lignite, totalling 8-12 meters in thickness. These sediments, include rich fossiliferous levels that contain plant impressions, seeds, molluscs, vertebrate remains and, of especial note, catarrhine primates" (Bernor et al., 2004).\r\nFor a more elaborate description of the sediments of the Rudabanya 2 sublocality see Andrews & Cameron (2010).
geology_comments: Deposition took place in different "lacustrine and peri-lacustrine" (swamp forests etc.) environments (Bernor et al., 2004) on a low-angle slope at the periphery of the Pannonian Lake. A detailed reconstruction of the paleoenvironments of Rudabánya is given in Andrews & Cameron (2010, Palaeogeogr., Palaeoclimatol., Palaeoecol. 297(2):311-329).
collection_methods: surface (in situ),sieve,field collection,survey of museum collection
collectors: G. Pethö, G. Hernyák, M. Kretzoi, L. Kordos, D.R. Begun, R. L. Bernor, M. Armour-Chelu, P. Andrews,
collection_comments: The first known vertebrate was found in 1902 by G. Pethö. A few important discoveries were made by G. Hernyák, the chief geologist of the Vilmos mine, in the 1960s and later on. First systematic collecting took place in the 1970s under the aegis of M. Kretzoi. After 1990 several paleontologists from the western hemisphere joined the excavations. For a more detailed review of the research history at Rudabánya see Bernor et al. (2004).\r\nAll or at least the bulk of the Rudabánya material is housed in the Geological and Geophysical Institute of Hungary (hung.: Magyar Földtani és Geofizikai Intézet, formerly Magyar Állami Földtani Intézet, abbreviated MAFI) in Budapest.
taxonomy_comments: The emphasis of the paper by Bernor et al. (2004) is on the Rudabánya 2 (also spelled "Rudabánya II") sublocality but most co-authors list taxa from all sublocalities without giving particularities on specimens and/or sites. List contains taxa that may have been cited by earlier authors under a different name and/or combination and some names cited in earlier works may have fallen into synonymy with those cited in the list (may already be noted in the list). Determinations in Bernor et al. (2004): E. Krolopp (molluscs); Z. Roček (amphibians); Z. Szyndlar (snakes); D. Janossy (birds); G. Topal (bats); L. Kordos and D. R. Begun (primates); G. Daxner-Höck, O. Fejfar, and L. Kordos (rodents); L. Werdelin (carnivorans); M. Gasparik (proboscideans); R. L. Bernor, M. Armour-Chely, T. Kaiser, R. Scott, L. de Bonis, J. Franzen, and K. Heissig (perissodactyls); M. Fortelius, R. L. Bernor, N. Fessaha, M. Armour-Chelu, and A. Gentry (artiodactyls). The list only contains vertebrate taxa mentioned in the text of Bernor et al. (2004) and ignores the faunal list given in table 3 (pp. 22-24) since it is partially contradictory to the informations given in the text (e.g. the table lists "?Leptotyphlopidae gen. et sp. indet." whereas Szyndlar in his section on snakes explicitly states that the only vertebra clearly referrable to Scolecophidia may belong to Typhlops and that the presence of leptotyphlopids in Rudabánya is very unlikely). In the taxon list in table 3 there appear 5 chelonian taxa which are not mentioned elsewhere in the paper: Trionyx sp., Geomyda sp., Testudo cf. kalksburgensis, Testudo sp. I, and Testudo sp. II.

geogcomments: Grotte Bodine is situated in a vertical cliff, comprising Lower Cretaceous limestones (Urgonian), which overhangs the Ardèche river at a height of 80 m, in the area of ‘Gorges de l’Ardèche’. The cave opens on the right bank of the river, 30 m below the surface of the plateau, and is situated 139 m above sea-level. Its opening is about 50 m above the river bed.

geogcomments: Shimokita Penninsula
stratcomments: No direct dating is available for the fossil localities, but the occurrence of marine vertebrates and the inclusion of marine-originated sediments indicate that the fossil-bearing layers had been deposited near the past sea level (Nakajima and Kuwano 1957; Nakajima 1958; Hasegawa et al. 1988). Therefore, the localities can be correlated to certain marine terrace surfaces Loc. 1 (~45 m above sea level) and Loc. 3 (~35 m) to the Tonamigaoka surface (35–45 m; MIS 5e).

geogcomments: The MIL 5 site was discovered in 2006 by a crane operator during commercial work in the area (Figure 1). The systematic excavations in 2007 yielded important findings such as the partial skeleton of M. borsoni and other fossils found about 14 m below the surface. The excavation followed the paleontological rules with the orientated block of squares, the reference point, the measure of the coordinates and the photographic documentation. The partial skeleton comprises two cranium fragments, a left maxilla fragment with M2, two complete upper tusks, an almost complete mandible with left and right m2 and m3, a thoracic vertebra spina, 13 ribs, right scapula, right and left humerus and radius, complete right ulna, pisiform and unciform. The two complete tusks were found in parallel position, but opposite direction (Figure 1): the tip of the one was near the pulp cavity of the other. Thus, we interpreted that the animal died nearby, the tusks were subsequently transported for several meters, and formed a barrier that sustained numerous of the bones of the skeleton.
primary_reference: E. Vlachos, E. Tsoukala, and E. Cregut-Bonnoure, C. Guerin, D. Mol. 2018. The Paradise Lost of Milia (Grevena, Greece; Late Pliocene, Early Villafranchian, MN15/MN16a): faunal composition and diversity. Quaternary MDPI 1(13):1-18

geology_comments: The Törökbálint Formation includes deposits from different palaeoenvironments including brackish to normal marine conditions (Korpás 1981; Nagymarosy 2012). The Mány Member, with a predominantly brackish mollusc community (e.g. Pirenella, Polymesoda, Potamides, Brotia; Báldi and Cságoly 1975), represents a transition between alluvial and normal marine conditions and is interpreted as a product of lagoonal environments (Báldi 1967; Nagymarosy and Gyalog 1997; Nagymarosy 2012).

geogcomments: The B-8 cave is a small cave close to B-9 (30T 692268 4741560), lying at an altitude of 2130 masl (Fig. 1). It consists of a horizontal passage 10 m in length, which drops 5 m at its end where a hole of 3 m diameter is located (Fig. 3). Fossiliferous sediment was found in the horizontal passage as well as at the bottom of the hole, where remains were much more abundant.
stratcomments: A bear sample was 14C dated in the Poznan Radiocarbon Laboratory, in Poland, yielding an age of 6930 ± 50 BP.

geogcomments: The Kitseli pothole is located near Alea village, in the Nemea area of the Peloponnese, South Greece\r\nThe name of the pothole is given by the present authors to honour the memory of Theodoros Kitselis who was a caver and first explored the caves of Alea, such as “Trypa tis Chionas”. Soon after the exploration of “Trypa tis Chionas” one of us (A.B.) explored the Kitseli pothole where he found the bones presented here\r\nThe maximum depth of the pothole is 10 m. It has a small entrance 40 X 80 cm (fig. 3). At the bottom of the pothole a small talus cone has been formed. The surface of the cave floor occupies an area of about 6 m2 and is full of stones and has a maximum length of 5 m and a minimum breadth of 0.70 m (fig. 1b). The pothole is decorated with a few stalactites, stalagmites, flowstones and cave corals. Very few recent bones, mainly belonging to artiodactyles, were noted on the surface.
primary_reference: E. Tsoukala, A. Batsiokas, and K. Chatzopoulou, G. Lazaridis. 2006. Quaternary mammalian remains from the Kitseli Pothole (Alea, Nemea, Peloponnese). Scientific Annals, School of Geology, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Special volume 98:273-284

lithdescript: The HAM 5 channel represents a riffle pool sequence of a small and shal-low meandering rivulet with a talweg width of 4–5 m and a maximum pool depth of 1 m. The gravelly bed load is composed exclusively of reworked pedogenic carbonate concretions that are typically 4–8 mm in diameter. Similar concretions are abundant in Bk palaeosol horizons of the bedrock, indicating a local source of HAM 5 rivulet

Detailed correlations between Swiss mammal levels and magnetostratigraphy were additionally published by Schlunegger et al. (1996), who clearly correlated the Rickenbach level (=MP29) within the chron 6 Cr, which actually corresponds to the interval 23.4–23.9 Ma (Fig. 1; Berger 2011)" (from Mennecart et al. 2012)\r\n\r\nde Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The age of the Rickenbach deposits, 23.9–23.4 Ma (berger 2011), corresponds to a major faunal remodeling known as the “Microbunodon Event” (scHerler et al. 2013, Mennecart 2015).
lithdescript: de Bonis and Mennecart 2017: The Rickenbach quarry (Canton of Solothurn, Swit- zerland, Fig. 1) was first mined during the 19th century for its fine sands of the Eocene filling a karstic fissure in Jurassic limestone. Soon after, Martin (1906) described the first remains of a latest Oligocene fauna. These fossils were embedded in 4 to 8 m molasse sandstone (Knauermolasse) overlaying the Eocene sediments. Mammal remains are especially concentrated in the basal conglomerate (Mennecart, pers. obs.).

stratcomments: Detritic-Carbonated Unit of Subirats - MN4; zone C from the Early Aragonian of the Calatayud-Daroca Basin,with an estimated age of 16.5–16.3 Ma\r\nConsidering these data, the dominantly reverse polarity interval recorded in the cores is best correlated to chron C5Br (15.974–15.160 Ma; boundaries after Ogg 2020). Coupled with the aforementioned correlation to Aragonian subzone Cb (16.15–15.94 Ma; Van der Meulen et al. 2012), this results in an estimated age of ~15.9 Ma for the site of els Casots.

collection_comments: The fossiliferous area of els Casots is huge, comprising all the fields around the farmhouses of Cal Solà and Cal Sutxet, but only a small area of about 6,200 m2 is protected. For the moment being, a small surface of just 400 m2 has been excavated, most of it during the 1990s field campaigns.\r\nField campaigns since 2018 have focused on the excavation of level J, a bone bed that has yielded partially articulated specimens. Furthermore, well-preserved carnivoran coprolites and numerous wood fragments have been found at level J.

geogcomments: The Furninha cave is a coastal karstic cavity in Lower Jurassic limestones, located ca. 850 m SE of Cape Carvoeiro, on the southern coast of the Peniche peninsula. This cave has an entrance of marine genesis located in the middle part of the coastal cliff, at ca. 15 m asl.
stratcomments: The sedimentary infilling of the cave was excavated in the late 19th century by Delgado, J. F. N., who defined two main stratigraphic units: a Holocene unit and a Pleistocene unit. Later, Breuil, H. and Zbyszewski, G. identified at the base of the lower unit (at 6 m asl) a marine stratigraphic level, which they ascribed to the Riss-Wurm interglacial. In the ca. 8 m-thick lower unit “Areias Quaternárias” (Quaternary Sands), with 7 main "níveis ossíferos" (bone rich levels) I to VII, were recognized, several separated by a travertine and nine of them rich in well-preserved faunal bones. Contains artifacts encompassing a sequence between the Middle and Upper Paleolithic, with assemblages pointing to the Acheulian, Mousterian, Gravettian, Solutrean and Magdalenian, most of them—especially the Mousterian industries—in association with abundant and diverse faunal remains. Another age, resulting from Uranium-series dating of a bone collected from the 3th stratigraphic level (8.5-9 m asl), is 80,880 + 42, 420 – 31, 260 years, but cannot be used as a reliable chronological marker due to the large standard deviation.
primary_reference: S. D. Figueiredo, P. P. Cunha, and I. S. Carvalho. 2022. Marine mammals fossil remains and synthesis of the sedimentary and paleontological record of the Furninha Cave Pleistocene (Peniche, Portugal). Cuaternario y Geomorfología
collection_comments: Repository: Geological Museum of LNEG (Laboratório Nacional de Energia e Geologia) in Lisbon.

geogcomments: López-Mendoza and Mena-Larraín 2011: Baño Nuevo-1 is located some 80 km to the northeast of the modern city of Coyhaique (45o17`S - 71o32`W), within an area that is characterized by large plains of mo- derate slopes traversed by several ravines including Arroyo Goichel and Arroyo Baño Nuevo that drain to the Ñirehuao river, one of the main tributaries of the Mañihuales river, part of the large basin of the Aysén river
stratcomments: López-Mendoza and Mena-Larraín 2011: The stratigraphic deposit of the cave has a depth of 150 cm (Figure 2b). From top to bottom, we can first rec- ognize a layer 1 that has very few cultural items, most of them due to perturbation processes such as those that -as we will see- have acted upon bones. Below it, we find sev- eral layers that are largely anthropogenic in origin. On the lower part of layer 3, the fragmented remains of ten human skeletons are found (Mena et al., 2003). Below this cultural layer and in a discordant fashion, we find layers 4 and 5 that are formed by rocky debris and collapse, and by organic clay and sand, dating to 10,500–9,000 BP and 10,500– 13,500 BP, respectively. Layer 4 corresponds to a complex and not yet well understood deposit (it encompasses a 1,500 years hiatus and at least one rock fall and active debris entry, which could have impinged on vertical migration of dermal bones, but not at the site’s scale). Layers 5 (and perhaps the lower section of 4 or 4B) are characterized by abundant bones of extinct species and by what seems to be degraded extinct ground sloth feces. Finally, Layer 6 corresponds to stratified sands belonging to a Pleistocene proglacial lake whose original date is unknown (it contacts the rocky foundations of the cave). This layer doesn`t have any faunal remains (not even diatoms or other microscopic organisms) and we only know it was deposited before 13,500 BP (Núñez et al., 2005).\r\n

collection_name: Page-Ladson Site Test F Combined Levels 23 and 26B
primary_reference: S.D. Webb and E. Simons. 2006. Vertebrate Paleontology.  In S.D. Webb (ed.), First Floridians and Last Mastodons: The Page-Ladson Site in the Aucilla River. Springer. 26:215-246
collection_comments:
From text:
We first sorted the taxa from each field season and cataloged them in the computerized system of the Florida Museum of Natural History collection of fossil vertebrates.

The two late Pleistocene localities that were most productive of fossil vertebrates were Levels 23 and 26 B in Test F. On October 30, 1992 toward the end of a very productive season, we took relatively large bulk samples from an area in which Levels 23 and 26 B both seemed unusually fossiliferous. The field number for the sample from Level 23 is FS 106 C. It weighed 3.04 kg after it was washed through 1/4 in. screen and dried. For Level 26 B the field number was FS 92.129 A and the bulk sample weighed 3.44 kg. In each case less than 10% of the bulk sample (fine sand and clay) washed through the screen. The material was then picked and sorted into four simple categories and weighed.

stratcomments: "In the sedimentation choke two separate, but correlated sections, have been excavated.  One section was excavated to the depth of 13 m, the other to 5 m, but neither of the excavations reached bedrock.  Based on the 14C dates of the bones and the 230Th/234U dates on the calcareous concretions, two bone bearing horizons were recognized; the Ursus horizon containing bones older than 70 ka and a 22 ka old Ursus maritimus horizon." (Hufthammer, 2001).
lithdescript: "In northern Norway, Lauritzen (1998) has investigated the stratigraphy of several of the many karst caves in Nordland county and the southern part of Troms county.  Fossiliferous sediments are rare in these caves, but from the Nordcemgrotta, Kjøpsvik (Text-fig. 2) a sample of approximately 450 bone fragments has been collected.  The cave is is a part of a conduit which leads to a paleospring.  It is almost completely filled with sediments that have been transported from deeper inside the cave system towards the spring.  The more than 13 m thick deposits consist of sand, gravel and diamictic sediments (Lauritzen et al., 1996)." (Hufthammer, 2001)
primary_reference: A. K. Hufthammer. 2001. The Weichselian (c. 115,000 - 10,000 B.P.) vertebrate fauna of Norway. Bollettino della Società paleontologica italiana 40:201-208
lithdescript.1: "In northern Norway, Lauritzen (1998) has investigated the stratigraphy of several of the many karst caves in Nordland county and the southern part of Troms county.  Fossiliferous sediments are rare in these caves, but from the Nordcemgrotta, Kjøpsvik (Text-fig. 2) a sample of approximately 450 bone fragments has been collected.  The cave is is a part of a conduit which leads to a paleospring.  It is almost completely filled with sediments that have been transported from deeper inside the cave system towards the spring.  The more than 13 m thick deposits consist of sand, gravel and diamictic sediments (Lauritzen et al., 1996)." (Hufthammer, 2001)
geology_comments: "According to Lauritzen et al. (1996) the fauna can be separated into two faunal complexes: an arctic (ice-contact) and a more temperate (forest) fauna.  In the former group they include the polar bear, arctic fox, the birds and also harp seal, while the latter complex includes the pine marten, the short tailed vole and, "to some degree", the wolf.  They suggest that the faunal elements from two different climatic episodes might have been mixed during sedimentation or that a climatic development took place during the fossil deposition and that the fauna represents and interstadial or an interglacial/interstadial transition, prior to 70±8.5 ka B.P." (Hufthammer, 2001)
taxonomy_comments: "From the Ursus horizon Lauritzen et al. (1996) reported bones of the mammals Ursus sp., Ursus maritimus, Canis lupus, Alopex lagopus, Vulpes vulpes, Martes martes, Microtus agrestis, Lemmus sp., the birds Somateria sp., Phalocrocorax carbo, Lagopus mutus, and the fish Gadus morhua." (Hufthammer et al., 2001)

geogcomments: The Gruta Nova da Columbeira cavity is located in Bombarral (Leiria, west central Portugal), on the southern slope of the deep steep Rôto Valley, scored by Columbeira Stream (tributary of Real River), and formed in Upper Jurassic limestones
stratcomments: Absolute dating carried out at the site offered an estimated chronological range between the MIS-5 and the early MIS-3 (35.9, 54.4, 60.9, and 101.5 ka BP) (Raposo & Cardoso, 1998) by U-series dating, the standard deviations being overly high. However, the main Neanderthal occupation corresponds to Level 8 (with the greatest turtle ensemble) whose U-series dating on a large bone of Dicerorhinus hemitoechus has offered a result of 87.1 ± 6.3 ka BP (Zilh˜ao et al., 2011).
primary_reference: I. B. Jiménez, J. L. Cardoso, and A. Pérez-García. 2023. The turtles from the middle Paleolithic site of Gruta Nova da Columbeira (Bombarral, Portugal): Update through an archaeozoological perspective. The Anatomical Record

stratcomments: Khersonian
lithdescript: Above, a layer of fine, micaceous sands, up to 6 to 8 m thick; 3" this layer of sand is crossed, towards the bottom, by a bed, sometimes doubled, a few decimeters thick, composed of gravel and puddingstone or breccia whose elements (flint or phosphate nodules) go up to the size of a fist at most; all cemented by an iron oxide with traces of manganese. It is this bed which is the most productive, as well as, for large pieces, the surrounding sand, mainly towards the contact with the clay;

geogcomments: The Pirro Nord 13 site (hereafter referred to as PN13) is one of the karst fissures found in the Pirro Nord quarry (locality also known as Cava Pirro or Cava Dell’Erba), located at the northwestern margin of the Gargano promontory, in the municipality of Apricena (Foggia, southern Italy)
stratcomments: The chronology of PN13 is based primarily on biochronological correlations of both large and small mammal assemblage, which indi cate an age confined between 1.6 and 1.3 Ma.
